text:
## Advice for Rope

Source URL: https://crash-restraint.com/advice

### Community conduct and playing with someone new

Categories: consent_scene_management, bottoming_skills

Do not touch people, their clothing, toys, or rope without explicit permission; do not interrupt or invite yourself into an active scene; do not assume that observing an activity grants you the same access; obtain consent and follow venue rules before photography; and use only labels a person accepts.

With a new partner, obtain local references, negotiate the planned activities, exchange relevant medical and mobility information, discuss known risks and injury, agree on safewords and check-ins, and prefer a public setting or a trusted observer. Warning signs in a partner include defensiveness about expertise, pressure to suppress self-advocacy, isolation from the community, controlling whom someone else may see, poor response to feedback, or blaming a bottom when a planned tie conflicts with that person's limits.

### Self-advocacy, warning signs, and emergency readiness

Categories: anatomy_injury_prevention, bottoming_skills, emergency_procedures

Speak up about discomfort or boundary violations, notify event staff promptly when appropriate, and choose ending a scene over risking injury. Evaluate proposed risks for yourself. Tingling, numbness, or unusual sensation calls for modification or removal; periodically compare normal feeling and strength. Light-headedness, visual spots, ringing ears, cold sweat, nausea, or incoherence can precede fainting or vomiting: prevent a fall, move to the floor and horizontal, loosen/remove rope, and obtain appropriate help.

Avoid strenuous rope when dehydrated, hungry, exhausted, ill, overheated, or very cold. Never leave a restrained person unattended, avoid neck loading that could choke, avoid impaired play, and do not suspend without training and appropriate equipment. Keep tested safety shears within reach and plan for sudden unconsciousness, fire, darkness, and evacuation. The handout highlights upper-arm rope, suspension, mixed/distraction play, outsiders joining a scene, and group play as circumstances needing extra caution. It also warns that past hardpoint use is not proof of present safety and that weak jute can fail as a main support line.

text:
## Lark's-Head Single Column: Original Photo Sequence

Source URL: https://crash-restraint.com/blog/2010/05/02/larks-head-based-single-column

### Construction and direction of pull

Categories: ties_knots_frictions, rope_handling

Form a lark's head on the column, reverse direction, and add wraps to the desired width. At the start, identify the interlocked single-line and doubled bights; pull the working rope through the doubled one. Reverse, lay a loop over the cuff, pass beneath the full cuff, and rise through that loop; dress and tighten. For a pull along the column, orient the under-cuff exit toward the pull. The opposite orientation can roll and bunch the cuff, levering the knot into tissue; reverse the finishing hitch when the pull direction changes.

## Reverse Somerville Bowline

Source URL: https://crash-restraint.com/blog/2011/05/31/reverse-somerville-bowline

### Why the reverse form exists

Categories: ties_knots_frictions, pattern_architecture

This difficult cuff solves a second-column problem where a simple pair of half hitches would load only the first wrap and cinch unless the rope returns to the harness. Reverse Somerville can end at the cuff or continue farther down the limb. It is slower, easier to overtighten, and vulnerable to unequal first-wrap loading, so it should not replace the normal Somerville by default. Its construction is video/photo dependent.

Source URL: https://crash-restraint.com/blog/2011/07/15/reverse-somerville-bowline-for-resistance-play

### Maintaining cuff space on a moving partner

Categories: ties_knots_frictions, consent_scene_management, anatomy_injury_prevention

The Reverse Somerville can be applied during negotiated active resistance because it does not require feeding a line beneath a completed cuff. The page's purpose is to preserve deliberate space rather than cinching tightly around a moving limb, especially when the cuff will remain or be suspended. The actual moving-target hand technique exists only in video/illustration and is not inferred.

text:
## Mechanical Advantage and Friction in Suspension Lifts

Source URL: https://crash-restraint.com/blog/2015/mechanical-advantage-and-friction-in-suspension-lifts

### Measured model results

Categories: rigging_mechanics, uplines_suspension_hardpoints, teaching_evaluation

Using hemp, sandbag loads, Black Diamond RockLocks, and a bathroom scale the author estimated ideal downward pull for a 100 lb load. With a bottom carabiner, modeled 1:1 through 5:1 pulls were 159, 97, 78, 70, and 65 lb; with rope-on-rope bottom contact, 159, 119, 103, 99, and 96 lb. With a 10% friction increase intended to approximate real use, values became 169/106/87/78/74 lb with a carabiner and 169/134/119/116/114 lb without. These are one experimental model, not certified design loads.

Consequences: use 3:1 when rope rubs rope; extra ratio mostly adds jams. With hardware, returns beyond 3:1 diminish rapidly but can improve control for a heavier bottom. Pulling upward avoids the top redirect and the model estimated 3:1 at 51 lb with a bottom carabiner and 70 lb without. Ergonomics may erase some gain, so one hand up/one down can help.

### Friction variables, holding, and uncertainty

Categories: rigging_mechanics, rope_handling

Off-axis orientation raised measured 3:1 effort about 5% with a carabiner and up to 15% on a thick ring. A ring much thicker than the rope added as much as about 20% in limited tests; it should not be assumed to protect weak rope. Reversing line directions through one carabiner added roughly 4–8% in 3:1 and about 10% in 5:1. Separate, well-spaced 5:1 carabiners reduced about another 10%; chaining them together did not. Typical accelerated lifts measured at least about 20% above steady motion. Synthetic-versus-natural friction changed 3:1 effort up to about 20%, while natural-fiber differences were smaller.

Observed holding force was about 10–15% of load for 3:1 with a bottom carabiner and 0–3% without (with jam risk); 5:1 was about 1–2% with hardware and jammed without. Small setup changes shifted results 5–10%, and direct friction measurements disagreed with the fitted coefficient. The author estimated scale repeatability near ±1 lb. These unresolved discrepancies reinforce using the tables for qualitative comparison, not safety rating.

text:
## Basic Suspension Safety

Source URL: https://crash-restraint.com/ties/123

Source level: 3

### Readiness and one-failure margin

Categories: uplines_suspension_hardpoints, teaching_evaluation, emergency_procedures

The page's governing principle is to remain more than one human or equipment error away from catastrophe. Use external redundancy where a single mistake cannot drop a person and internal redundancy through pauses, identification checks, and abort plans. Being able to reproduce a harness is not suspension readiness; readiness is calm response when events leave the planned path and enough floor-rope fluency that knots and feeding consume little emergency attention.

Early suspensions should have nearby assistance and an experienced monitor, keep a static chest line to the ring throughout, leave arms free during entry/exit, avoid high-nerve-risk or highly painful ties, and use a mat. An experienced bottom can recognize abnormal loading and improve feedback, but does not transfer the rigger's responsibility. Feeling chronically rushed, adrenalized, awkward, focused on the next technical step instead of the partner, or unable to recover from a small deviation indicates that the chosen difficulty exceeds current capacity.

### Preflight and informed consent

Categories: consent_scene_management, bottoming_skills, anatomy_injury_prevention

Check present food, water, illness, sleep, soreness, bathroom needs, and temperature. Ask about medical conditions, mobility limits, current and old injuries, allergies, medication, and emergency devices; learn where an inhaler or epinephrine device is and how the owner expects help. Ask which prior positions worked, the hardest previous tie, injuries, and bad scenes.

Explain likely skin impressions, bruising/petechiae, burn, nerve compression, fainting, hardpoint uncertainty, drop risk, and possible equipment failure. The page reports impressions often fading within hours, bruising/petechiae sometimes lasting weeks, and severe burns months; these are author-provided ranges, not medical guarantees. Practice motor/sensory checks. At the space, inspect the hardpoint, identify trip and human hazards, and locate visible reachable cutters.

Mats protect head and joints but can slide, trip at an edge, or be too soft for balance. Large interlocking flooring reduces walking; the author favors dense wrestling/gym mats roughly 2–5 cm (1–2 in) for durable cleanable public use. Those dimensions are preference, not a universal impact rating.

### Control during changes

Categories: uplines_suspension_hardpoints, rigging_mechanics, rope_handling

Protect the head first. For beginning technique, attach the chest to the ring before other loading and keep it attached. Before changing a support line, positively identify the rope and every component it influences; predict the sequence of body forces; ask whether a mistake could put the head on the floor; detach any head/neck support before changing other geometry; and decide how to abort mid-change. Run this check while the line is still secured, not after it is fully opened.

Use completion of a change—and the urge to “do something” after a quiet period—as prompts to check sensation, strength, consciousness, and comfort. Anticipate that sex or impact play can distort time and attention; choose a safer rope system when distraction is planned.

### Fainting, falls, and exit

Categories: emergency_procedures, anatomy_injury_prevention, bottoming_skills

Watch for light-headedness, spots, ringing ears, cold sweat, nausea, or unexpected incoherence. Prevent a fall and move toward horizontal. A conscious person may first sit with knees raised; an unconscious person or someone not improving seated should be laid flat. If consciousness does not promptly return, check breathing/circulation, use an appropriate recovery position when indicated, and summon emergency medical care. These source steps are not a substitute for current first-aid training. The author's suggestion that an experienced pair might continue after early symptoms fully resolve is source-specific and should not be treated as medical consensus.

After a fall with possible spine or head injury, the page says not to move the person before medical assistance unless another immediate danger requires it. Bring an inverted/horizontal person upright gradually. The author keeps the chest line lightly loaded for at least about one minute after a standing exit because delayed fainting can occur; that timing is his practice, not a guaranteed safe interval. Stay close until cognition and balance return; do not allow immediate driving, climbing, or unassisted walking while the person remains altered.

## Swiss Seat

Source URL: https://crash-restraint.com/ties/129

Source level: 3

### Beginner hip harness and line options

Categories: pattern_architecture, uplines_suspension_hardpoints, accessibility_adaptation

This old climbing-seat adaptation is fast and usually comfortable for seated or face-up support; it can invert but is not the author's preferred inversion harness. A seat alone tends to rotate a person backward, so connect the chest to the ring before loading. Support may attach to leg loops, hip bands, or a hanger sharing both; each moves the support vector relative to center of gravity and changes body angle.

> Media limitation: harness construction and hanger formation are video-only.

## Attaching Support Lines (Half-Tatu Hitch)

Source URL: https://crash-restraint.com/ties/135

Source level: 3

### Attachment variants and lowering rehearsal

Categories: uplines_suspension_hardpoints, ties_knots_frictions, emergency_procedures

The Half-Tatu attachment can connect one or several harness points, with or without a carabiner. In the carabiner form, the initial three aligned bights are not stable against the body until the line returns from the top and passes through the carabiner; practice the configuration's behavior when lowering past the original lift height over a mat. More bights can create a multi-point hanger.

> Media limitation: this tie page points to a photo article; the full written/photo sequence and center-bight failure warning are retained in the 2012 blog record.

text:
## Securing Support Lines: Wrap and Slipped Hitch

Source URL: https://crash-restraint.com/ties/136

Source level: 3

### Secure, controllable lockoff

Categories: uplines_suspension_hardpoints, ties_knots_frictions, rigging_mechanics

The intended method can be opened controllably under high load without jamming. Do not half-hitch around the entire bundle: that hitch can rotate and release the preceding wraps. Split the bundle so the first half hitch cannot rotate, and ensure the pulled bight actually forms a half hitch around part of the bundle, creating a stable-but-unlocked pause during release.

Lock the slipped hitch with a second, unslipped half hitch unless the line is under immediate hands-on adjustment, closely watched, and not protecting the head. Do not lock merely by passing the working end through the slip bight; the bight can retract into the bundle and trap the end under load. The method can be made at the ring or lower on the line.

> Media limitation: exact wrap count and hand transfers are shown visually.

text:
## Rope Material Selection

Source URL: https://crash-restraint.com/ties/2

### How to interpret the comparison

Categories: rope_materials_maintenance, teaching_evaluation

Material labels do not determine a rope's properties by themselves. Fiber quality, lay, braid, diameter, treatment, and supplier can dominate. The site's rankings for roughness, suppleness, knot holding, friction, burn tendency, cleaning, maintenance, and life are subjective comparison bands rather than linear measurements. Stretch is omitted because construction, elastic versus permanent extension, strength, and applied load make fiber-only comparisons misleading. Check manufacturer data and handle a sample before buying a full kit.

### Synthetic fibers

Categories: rope_materials_maintenance, rope_handling

Source-reported approximate properties for nominal 6 mm rope are:

| Fiber | Source breaking-strength range | Handling and care claims |
| --- | ---: | --- |
| Nylon | 1,200–1,500 lb | Soft, supple, moderately slippery; tolerates gentle machine washing and hanging dry; can be dyed, but blends may dye unevenly. Quality and service life vary greatly. |
| MFP | 1,100–1,300 lb | Ties much like nylon, comes in many colors, and is not readily home-dyed; source lists 5+ years. |
| Spun polyester | about 1,200 lb | Moderate texture/friction, strong and long-lived; POSH is a natural-colored example. Dyeing polyester is difficult and chemically demanding. |
| Filament polyester | about 2,000 lb | Hard, very slippery, poor knot holding, rugged outdoors, washable, and long-lived; the site uses narrow 3/16-in line for a distinctive feel. |
| Filament polypropylene | 1,000–1,200 lb | Very harsh and stiff despite floating; the source rates it unsuitable for body bondage. |
| Hempex | about 1,100 lb | Washable, synthetic hemp look, moderate handling; decorative fuzz melts rather than singes. |

These are site-compiled ranges, not guarantees for an unlabelled rope. Use the actual product rating for load-bearing decisions.

### Natural and specialty fibers

Categories: rope_materials_maintenance, aesthetics_performance

The site's nominal 6 mm comparisons report hemp around 400–500 lb, jute around 200–300 lb, and cotton roughly 200–600 lb. Hemp often softens with use, handles washing better than jute, may need tensioned drying, and can become sticky in humidity; some common hemp has a chunky photographic lay. Jute is lighter, more supple, quick to feed, and higher-friction, but is fragile wet, requires re-treatment after washing, and has a shorter reported service life. Three-strand, coreless cotton can be a soft inexpensive starter rope, while common loose cotton can jam; avoid cheap cored cotton.

Flax/linen is described as combining hemp-like physical feel with a jute-like visual line. Silk and rayon sold as “bamboo” are soft specialty options with incomplete data. Alpaca is exceptionally soft but delicate and hard to clean. Coconut and sisal are intentionally harsh sensation materials rather than ordinary restraint rope. The page repeats community concerns that manila can be chemically harsh and splintering, while noting the author had not tested it. Material choice must follow the desired sensation, maintenance burden, visibility, and actual construction—not tradition alone.

### Cleaning, water, and dye

Categories: rope_materials_maintenance

The page treats nylon, MFP, and filament polyester as water-compatible and washable; hemp shrinks and can jam wet; jute may be used wet only with damage risk and must not remain wet. Hemp, jute, cotton, and flax can accept suitable dyes, while polyester and MFP are difficult to dye at home. The author favors fiber-reactive dyes for plant fibers and acid dye for nylon. Always reconcile this general guidance with the maker's care instructions and re-inspect rope after any wet processing.

text:
## Suspension Equipment Recommendations

Source URL: https://crash-restraint.com/ties/241

Source level: 3

### Carabiners, rings, and plates

Categories: rigging_mechanics, uplines_suspension_hardpoints, rope_materials_maintenance

For most uses choose a rated aluminum climbing carabiner. Evaluate usable width and flatness at the basket, rounded cross-section for lower friction/wear, and a lock operable with one hand. The author's preference is the Black Diamond RockLock TwistLock; this is a personal recommendation, not the only safe choice.

Larger rings accept more lines and hands but consume height. Thicker rings add weight/friction and are not proven to prevent rope break; wood creates more friction than metal. A small three-hole rigging plate spaces carabiners horizontally, reduces jams, and conserves height. Larger plates tip more and crowd neighboring connectors; the source reserves them for complex multi-bottom work.

### Swivels, tools, and frames

Categories: rigging_mechanics, emergency_procedures, uplines_suspension_hardpoints

A shackle/openable swivel mounted directly to a plate saves one carabiner of height; read the specific manual and opening limitations. Carry shears regardless of any rigging knife. A locking, fully tapered marlinspike may open a jam, but cutting is faster when release is urgent.

The author describes a timber A-frame made from swing-set brackets. His example has 90 in floor-to-beam clearance, a beam-parallel footprint equal to beam length plus 48 in, a 97 in perpendicular footprint, and 103 in legs. These dimensions are one build, not a plan. Quoted bracket guidance limits 4×4 legs to 8 ft unless feet are set in concrete, a 4×6 beam to 12 ft and three swings, and total load to 500 lb; verify the current manufacturer's instructions and engineer human-suspension use rather than treating these numbers as approval.

## Ankles-to-Chest Hogtie

Source URL: https://crash-restraint.com/ties/43

Source level: 1

### Modular sequence

Categories: pattern_architecture, ties_knots_frictions

Apply a Quick Shoulder Harness or Shinju. Join the wrists with a separate short rope and the ankles with a separate long rope. Attach the ankle rope to the back of the shoulder harness with a slipped half hitch, then connect the wrists to the stem between ankles and chest. Separate components make the tension easier to adjust or replace.

text:
# Historical evidence digest: aramid-fiber rope sling fatigue and weatherability (NBSIR 76-1159)

## Use boundary: read this first

This is a manually written evidence digest of a **September 1976 prototype test program for helicopter external-cargo sling legs**. It is not a current equipment standard, an operational manual, or a validation of any rope system. The central result is that the manufacturer-installed end fittings were inadequate: those inadequate end fittings prevented a thorough evaluation of the rope bodies and left the report with observations rather than fully substantiated conclusions. [NBSIR-76-1159: title page; abstract; §8 Concluding Remarks]

Nothing here transfers the prototype findings to bondage rope, human suspension, natural-fiber rope, knots, uplines, body contact, care or retirement rules, load ratings, end-fitting construction, or helicopter operation. The source studied aramid-fiber prototype sling legs with manufacturer-installed industrial terminations. Its material, construction, termination, loading, and service context differ from those other domains. [NBSIR-76-1159: §1 Introduction; §2 Specimens]

The digest intentionally omits numerical loads, capacity ratings, loading recipes, fitting dimensions, apparatus construction details, vendor claims, obsolete procurement information, photographs, figures, and source tables. The retained value is historical and methodological: it shows how termination failures, sparse sampling, test-control anomalies, and uncombined environmental stresses can prevent apparently extensive rope tests from answering the intended material question. [NBSIR-76-1159: §3 Preliminary Tensile Tests; §4 Fatigue Studies; §5 Simulated Weathering Studies; §7 Discussion]

## Source identity and date

The report is *Fatigue and Weatherability Studies of Aramid-Fiber Rope Slings*, NBSIR 76-1159, by Nixon Halsey and Leonard Mordfin of the National Bureau of Standards. The report itself is dated September 1976 and identifies a U.S. Army air-mobility laboratory as sponsor. Its DOI is `10.6028/NBS.IR.76-1159`. The current NIST landing page lists the same authors, report number, and DOI, while giving January 1, 1976 as its publication date; the September date is the one printed on both the report title page and bibliographic sheet. [NIST-LANDING: publication metadata; NBSIR-76-1159: title page; bibliographic data sheet]

The work belonged to a program seeking lighter, easier-to-handle external-cargo slings for helicopters. This phase explored whether a then-new synthetic aramid material might be useful. It was a prototype-development study, not certification of a finished sling family and not a general study of rope. [NBSIR-76-1159: §1 Introduction]

## What was tested

The program tested 26 prototype sling-leg specimens. Each specimen combined an aramid-fiber rope body with an attached end fitting, which means the experimental object was the complete specimen rather than bare rope. That distinction is decisive because the terminations often governed the observed failure. [NBSIR-76-1159: abstract; §2 Specimens; §8 Concluding Remarks]

The specimen set crossed two rope configurations with three nominal capacity families. In the **parallel-strand** configuration, individually formed strands were gathered without cabling the collection together. In the **cabled-strand** configuration, strands were assembled around a core in a cabled structure. The report labels the capacity families low, medium, and high; this digest preserves those category names but not their historical numerical ratings. Jacket materials and end-fitting variants introduced further differences, yielding six basic rope styles and nine specimen styles. [NBSIR-76-1159: §2 Specimens; §2.2 Parallel-Strand Rope Construction; §2.3 Cabled-Strand Rope Construction; §2.4 Jackets; §2.5 End Fittings]

The ropes were aramid-fiber constructions with protective jackets. Jacket families included treated braid, extruded polymer, and untreated braid. Those differences matter as experimental confounders: configuration, capacity family, jacket, fitting, and potting practice were not varied as one clean, balanced factorial design. A result associated with one specimen style therefore cannot automatically be assigned to the rope configuration alone. [NBSIR-76-1159: §2 Specimens; §2.4 Jackets; §7.3 Weatherability]

All specimens arrived with manufacturer-installed potted compression-type terminations. Some fittings were commercial types associated with other rope classes; a small subset used experimental fittings. The report's own discussion warns that a fitting developed for steel wire rope or a lower-modulus synthetic rope should not be presumed suitable for aramid rope. This is retained only as an explanation of confounding in the historical experiment, not as fitting-selection or design advice. [NBSIR-76-1159: §2.5 End Fittings; §7.4 End Fittings]

## The study paths

### Preliminary tensile tests

The preliminary tests loaded specimens repeatedly and then continued to failure. Every tested specimen failed near an end fitting. Potting resin had moved beyond the fitting into adjacent fibers and then cured, removing normal flexibility at the transition. Because the termination region failed first, the tests did not determine the true strength of the rope bodies and could not support a strength comparison between parallel- and cabled-strand constructions. [NBSIR-76-1159: §3 Preliminary Tensile Tests; §7.1 Breaking Loads]

This is a classic censored-measurement problem. A machine can record a precise failure load while still failing to measure the target property. Here, the observed endpoint was often the capacity of a defective rope-to-fitting transition, not the intrinsic capacity of the rope free length. The report accordingly treated even apparently strong results cautiously. [NBSIR-76-1159: §3 Preliminary Tensile Tests; §7.1 Breaking Loads]

### Increasing-load-spectrum fatigue

One fatigue path used three successively more demanding blocks. Five specimens entered this path. Only one completed it without failure; the other four stopped early because of end-fitting failures rather than identified rope-body deficiencies. The automatic spectrum programmer also malfunctioned, so some specimens received cycle histories that deviated from the specified spectra. Both facts weaken cross-specimen comparisons: the dominant failure site was outside the intended material question, and the delivered histories were not perfectly uniform. [NBSIR-76-1159: §4.1 Increasing-Load-Spectrum Tests]

The defensible conclusion is not that four ropes failed the spectrum. It is that four complete sling-leg specimens failed, with the failures attributed to terminations. The rope-body fatigue endurance for those specimens remained unresolved. [NBSIR-76-1159: §4.1 Increasing-Load-Spectrum Tests; §7.2 Fatigue Endurance]

### Alternating-block-spectrum fatigue

The second fatigue path alternated a repeated baseline block with brief higher-load interruptions. Seven specimens entered this path: two parallel-strand and five cabled-strand. One parallel-strand specimen pulled out of a fitting prematurely, while the other completed its test. Among the cabled-strand specimens, three completed without observed failure, one sustained a partial strand failure but completed the scheduled history, and one developed a free-length fatigue failure relatively early. [NBSIR-76-1159: §4.2 Alternating-Block-Spectrum Tests]

These mixed outcomes show why survivor counts alone are inadequate. "Completed," "partial rope damage," "free-length failure," and "termination failure" represent different evidence states. Pooling them into a single pass/fail number would erase the distinction between an interpretable rope event and a censored termination event. [NBSIR-76-1159: §4.2 Alternating-Block-Spectrum Tests; §7.2 Fatigue Endurance]

Across both fatigue paths, twelve specimens were fatigue-loaded. Only one failed to endure its imposed spectrum because of damage identified in the rope itself; the others either completed their histories or stopped at an end-fitting inadequacy. That single free-length event did not establish a construction-wide weakness because another specimen of the same broad configuration and capacity family completed the same spectrum. The study also lacked high-capacity parallel-strand fatigue specimens, preventing a direct configuration comparison in that family. [NBSIR-76-1159: §7.2 Fatigue Endurance]

### Residual-strength follow-up

Seven specimens that had completed or otherwise survived their fatigue histories were later pulled in tension. Two then failed in the rope free length, although one had already shown strand damage during fatigue and the other may have been damaged by recoil from a fitting failure. The other five failed in or near an end fitting. Thus most follow-up tests again measured a termination-limited endpoint rather than clean post-fatigue rope residual strength. [NBSIR-76-1159: §4.3 Residual Strength Tests]

The report could not turn these follow-ups into a robust residual-strength distribution. Prior damage state differed among survivors, the survival process selected which specimens reached follow-up, and the terminations censored most final tests. A survivor that later reaches a high force before a fitting event is not proof that the rope retained a particular fraction of its original strength. [NBSIR-76-1159: §4.3 Residual Strength Tests; §7.2 Fatigue Endurance]

### Simulated weathering

Seven specimens were held under tension during accelerated cycles combining moisture, heat, and ultraviolet exposure. The weathering and fatigue programs used different specimens, so the study did not test their combined effect. The environmental apparatus also changed after the first test when unexpectedly high temperatures were observed, and the source records additional timing or airflow anomalies for individual runs. These controls make the weathering series exploratory rather than a uniform exposure dataset. [NBSIR-76-1159: §5 Simulated Weathering Studies; §5.1 Test Equipment; §7.5 Synergistic Effects]

Three specimens, all parallel-strand varieties, pulled out at the end fitting exposed inside the chamber. Their potting material had softened excessively during the simulated environment. Four specimens completed the exposure. When those four were subsequently tension-tested, only two failures occurred in the rope free length and therefore reflected rope residual strength more directly. The other failure modes did not support a clean comparison between configurations. [NBSIR-76-1159: §5.2 Tests and Results; §7.3 Weatherability]

One weathered cabled-strand specimen with a braided jacket showed visible fiber discoloration in the exposed region; the report did not observe the same change in the other specimens. That observation was based on one specimen style and was entangled with jacket differences. It is evidence of a particular historical observation, not a general weathering law or a visual retirement criterion. [NBSIR-76-1159: §5.2 Tests and Results; §7.3 Weatherability]

### Permanent elongation

The report calculated permanent or quasi-permanent elongation from free-length measurements taken around the tensile, fatigue, weathering, and follow-up tests. It observed length changes after loading, a tendency for larger changes with larger rope diameter, and a qualitative tendency for fatigue-related elongation to be greater in the cabled-strand group than in the parallel-strand group. Weathered specimens also exhibited creep-like length change. [NBSIR-76-1159: §6 Permanent Elongation]

The authors explicitly described these elongation conclusions as approximate and unsubstantiated because the dataset was sparse. Measurement opportunities and histories differed, and the design was not adequate to isolate configuration, diameter, treatment, and termination effects. Accordingly, elongation in this report is a descriptive outcome, not a service limit, inspection threshold, care instruction, or retirement rule. [NBSIR-76-1159: §6 Permanent Elongation]

## Why the end fittings control the interpretation

The report identifies several termination-related mechanisms. Potting material could migrate into the adjacent rope and cure into a stiff transition. Environmental exposure could soften a different potting system enough to permit pullout. A comparatively hard potting material could leave a sharp stiffness transition and local stress concentration even without visible seepage. Failures appeared adjacent to fittings, within fittings, and by pullout. These are distinct mechanisms, but each can prevent a test from reaching a rope-body endpoint. [NBSIR-76-1159: §3 Preliminary Tensile Tests; §5.2 Tests and Results; §7.4 End Fittings]

The study therefore does not support ranking parallel-strand against cabled-strand rope by intrinsic breaking strength. It also does not establish that either construction met a general fatigue or weatherability requirement. Most failures were directly attributable to fitting inadequacies, only 26 prototypes were tested, some cells were absent, and the remaining rope-body events were too sparse and inconsistent for complete evaluation. [NBSIR-76-1159: §7.1 Breaking Loads; §7.2 Fatigue Endurance; §7.3 Weatherability; §8 Concluding Remarks]

The authors offered cautiously favorable observations about what future, adequately terminated prototypes might be capable of, but they explicitly framed them as observations offered without fully substantiated conclusions. Those conditional statements are not evidence that the tested specimens were qualified, and they are not modern recommendations. [NBSIR-76-1159: §8 Concluding Remarks]

## Evidence that can and cannot be carried forward

The durable methodological lesson is that a rope test must classify *where and how* failure occurs before using the result to infer a rope-body property. Termination-limited failures are not interchangeable with free-length failures. Test-history deviations, prior damage, survivor selection, jacket differences, missing comparison cells, and separately applied stressors must remain visible in any analysis. [NBSIR-76-1159: §4 Fatigue Studies; §5 Simulated Weathering Studies; §7 Discussion]

The historical evidence supports only a narrow account: these particular aramid-fiber prototype sling legs showed a mixture of termination failures, a small number of rope-body fatigue events, a few interpretable residual-strength events, visible weathering in one specimen style, and approximate permanent elongation. The program did not isolate a universal material law. [NBSIR-76-1159: §4.3 Residual Strength Tests; §6 Permanent Elongation; §8 Concluding Remarks]

Do not use this digest to choose equipment, set a load, construct an end fitting, plan an aircraft lift, judge body-contact safety, or infer the performance of a modern rope. Any such use would exceed both the study design and this corpus's admitted scope. [NBSIR-76-1159: §1 Introduction; §8 Concluding Remarks]

## Citation key

- **NIST-LANDING** — Current NIST publication landing page for NBSIR 76-1159, accessed July 16, 2026.
- **NBSIR-76-1159** — Halsey, Nixon, and Leonard Mordfin, *Fatigue and Weatherability Studies of Aramid-Fiber Rope Slings*, National Bureau of Standards Interim Report 76-1159, September 1976, DOI `10.6028/NBS.IR.76-1159`.

This digest is a manual paraphrase. It does not reproduce the source PDF, page scans, photographs, figures, or tables.

text: ## Chest structures: Days 15–21

text:
### Horizontal ladders, diagonal weaving, and diamonds

Source: https://rope365.com/chestladder/

A ladder harness uses broad horizontal wraps, commonly above and below pectoral tissue, joined by a stem; shoulder straps resist downward slippage. Opening the front stem into a V may move pressure away from clavicles, while the same framework can draw a star or other shape. Keep wraps flat and evenly tensioned, test the stem and wraps in all expected directions, and adapt placement to breathing and anatomy. Arms-up construction can become tighter when arms lower because the rib cage changes shape.

Source: https://rope365.com/weavings/

Diagonal lines can be woven over and under at crossings. Individual lines remain mobile, but collectively the fabric-like field gains stability. The teardrop is one application; the general exercise is to choose a diagonal origin, keep spacing and tension regular, then test the harness through breath, arm movement, spinal flexion, and different body shapes. Weaving is not the same as a compact friction: stability emerges from the entire field.

Source: https://rope365.com/chest-diamond/

An open-diamond/karada begins with a central sequence of knots or hitches and opens the paired lines sideways. Opening progressively adds chest tension, so leave allowance and monitor breathing. The between-the-legs route requires explicit agreement; a waist route is an alternative. Diamonds may also be drawn with half hitches and crossing hitches or by overlaying opposing spirals. Angled load can feel gentler than a straight ladder, but lines near the collarbones and neck still require careful placement.

text:
### Decorative knots, serpentine release, and asymmetry

Source: https://rope365.com/chest-knots/

Two half knots make either a square or granny knot depending on orientation. The square is generally easier to untie; the granny is less predictable, and neither is a critical-load knot. A double-coin/Josephine knot is related to the carrick-bend family and joins two ropes in a broad decorative form. A triple-crown button knot supplies loops or “petals.” Decorative knots add bulk and pressure, so compactness, placement, and an exit remain functional concerns.

Source: https://rope365.com/serpentine/

A serpentine catches horizontal lines alternately around a central stem. If deliberately constructed for it, withdrawing the stem can collapse the structure. Equal spacing and progressive stem tension make the lightning-like form predictable. Lines along the lower ribs can affect diaphragm motion or press the solar plexus; map the rib edge and individual response before loading that area.

Source: https://rope365.com/chestasymmetry/

Asymmetry can begin off-center, distort an initially symmetric harness, or balance unequal lines into a stable whole. Inspect from several angles, test movement, and distinguish visual imbalance from uneven or hazardous tension. It is also a practical way to accommodate one-sided injury or mobility.

text:
### Shaping rather than merely decorating

Source: https://rope365.com/sculpture/

Rope can frame, lift, compress, or redirect soft tissue and can support feminine, masculine, or personally meaningful forms. A chest binder needs an underlying vertical structure and broad lines that move tissue upward before compressing it toward the rib cage, avoiding folded or twisted skin. Long-duration binding has considerations beyond a short scene. Define the desired identity, sensation, and marks together, make several temporary studies, and choose the version the person wearing it actually values.

text:
## Hogties and whole-body links: Days 36–42

Source: https://rope365.com/hogtie-wrists-to-ankles/

A minimal hogtie connects wrists and ankles with one or more cuffs and a locked joining line. Minimalism concentrates load on wrists: place cuffs on forearms, retain enough room for circulation but not accidental escape, and test median/ulnar hand closing plus radial sensation and hand opening. Separate versus shared wrist and ankle cuffs change access and restriction. Pulling the connector upward is a new load case and requires a fresh inspection.

Source: https://rope365.com/hogtie-chest-harness/

Attaching ankles to a chest harness or box-tie stem spreads force over a broader structure. Chest-loaded harnesses more often affect breathing; arm-loaded box ties more often affect nerves. Attach at several candidate points and observe slippage and distribution rather than assuming the central stem is always best. Connecting lines must avoid exposed nerves.

Source: https://rope365.com/hogtie-shoulders-to-ankles/

Shoulder straps can take pressure that would otherwise reach wrists. A single-column around both shoulders or a bight-built pair can form the base, with underarm lines pressed toward torso rather than into the armpit. An armbinder/strappado then places arms parallel behind the back and carries significant median, ulnar, radial, and internal shoulder-mechanics risk. Keep wraps broad and modest, check thumb/index closing, ring/little-finger closing, and full hand opening, adjust armpit skin, and return arms forward slowly at weakness. Favor a quick exit and be ready to cut.

Source: https://rope365.com/hogtie-ankles-crossing/

Crossed ankles force knees apart. Leave room because changing ankle angle can tighten a shared cuff painfully. A chest structure or folded-leg ties may hold the position without excessive ankle pressure. Test knees together and apart and ask whether the position can be achieved without loading the ankle bones directly.

Source: https://rope365.com/hogtie-legs-crossing/

Crossing knees and then positioning ankles introduces torsion through hips, knees, and ankles. Find the position without rope first and never force a knee to accept rotation. Use separate lines if needed to maintain angles without one over-tight junction, compare left-over-right and right-over-left, and keep the minimum structure that reliably holds.

Source: https://rope365.com/hogtie-asymmetry/

Asymmetry may come from different limb ties, body position, tension, or micro-bondage at feet and toes. Small digits need gentler pressure and easy inspection. Deliberately unequal sensation is different from an unintentionally overloaded side; move and load the tie to reveal the difference.

Source: https://rope365.com/hogtie-flipping/

Gravity can turn a loose supine hogtie into a tight prone one. Begin with enough slack for the most demanding orientation, use a bed or mat without an edge to fall from, and pivot through a knee or elbow rather than loading a wrist or ankle. In asymmetry, each roll direction creates a different pinning and pressure pattern, so evaluate both before play.

text: ## Practice curriculum: upper- and lower-body positions

text:
### Hands in front and behind

Source: https://rope365.com/hands-in-front/

Front positions are generally more accessible and sustainable. Explore: straight parallel arms in a front armbinder; crossed or parallel wrists anchored near belly; horizontal forearms in a reverse box/straightjacket tie; palms together at chest in front prayer; crossed arms over chest; a rope-held self-hug; and wrists near face with forearms either together or independent. Compare elbow mobility, wrist rotation, breath, and escapability rather than presuming “in front” means harmless.

Source: https://rope365.com/hands-in-the-back/

Warm shoulders and find each person's range before binding behind. Bunny ears place wrists behind the head and may join crossed, praying, separate, or parallel forearms. Reverse prayer with fingers upward is demanding; palms out, fingers sideways, or fingers downward offer different and sometimes more accessible versions. Wrists may attach low at hips or butt. Strappado holds arms parallel and can create internal shoulder/latissimus nerve pressure even with good rope placement, so monitor hands and release fast. An upper-body stem can also draw bent elbows toward the torso.

text:
### Elbows, asymmetry, and indirect neck restriction

Source: https://rope365.com/elbow-ties/

Elbow-oriented positions include: upper arms together behind with wrists free or apart; a waitress pose with elbows behind and wrists in front; wrists in front while elbows are pulled back; parallel bent “T-Rex/mantis” arms; demon/tengu wrists near shoulders with elbows down; angel/tenshi elbows drawn back like wings; and forearms around the neck. Arm and shoulder warm-up, loose tension, nerve checks, and individual range are essential. A forearm self-choke needs enough wrist separation that the neck remains free and a rehearsed immediate release.

Source: https://rope365.com/asymmetric-arms/

Pull joined wrists toward different points around chest, hip, or shoulder; leave one arm free in a one-arm box tie; position one chicken-wing arm; combine one elbow up with the other down in a riffle/teppō; or draw a strappado to one side. An upward elbow fights gravity and fatigues, so the upper arm requires especially quick release. One-sided forms are valuable adaptations for injury and asymmetric mobility, not merely visual effects.

Source: https://rope365.com/neck-restriction-ties/

This chapter intentionally catalogs dangerous indirect neck arrangements: arms together in front connected near the neck, knuckles or palms against neck, one or two arms wrapped around it, forearms pressing from both sides, and elbow-up positions that tighten the contact. It instructs readers to study breathing and neck anatomy first, negotiate non-verbal signals, keep the cutter close, avoid a fixed tight neck line, never work alone, and release immediately. These designs can become lethal from body movement even when no rope initially compresses the airway or vessels.

text:
### Build upper-body attachment structures

Source: https://rope365.com/upper-body-structures/

Shoulder straps prevent torso wraps from falling and, when doubled, distribute force more broadly. A stem becomes stronger when wrapped, when its bight is integrated, or when surrounded by a tight net such as a kikkō field. A second stem can join two wraps on the opposite side, giving front and back attachment options. A waist line resists vertical sliding and adds an anchor but also affects abdominal breathing. Mountain/tasuki Xs connect shoulder straps, while diamonds redirect load obliquely and form attachment nets. Each principle can be symmetric or asymmetric and should be load-tested in the direction it will actually be used.

text:
### Hips, legs, sitting, and combined restrictions

Source: https://rope365.com/hips-and-butt/

A hip harness combines waist, hips, and upper thighs for decoration or attachment. It may extend into an upper-body harness, add side handles for asymmetric pulls, frame or compress buttocks, or form a two-column “booty basket” between waist and thighs. A side basket joins waist and one leg; a monoblock/package folds both legs toward waist. These wide structures can be strong enough to move a person, but strength also means deep hip/groin nerve pressure is possible. Test each handle direction and keep crotch contact separately negotiated.

Source: https://rope365.com/lower-body-structures/

Lower-body studies include: a rope skirt around thighs; a full-length mermaid with parallel or crossed legs; a calf binder; decorative “stockings” around individual legs; crossed-ankle/agura sitting; soles-together butterfly; and lotus, shoelace, or side-crossed variations. Offset ankle bones rather than stacking malleoli against one another. A straight-leg wrap may become far tighter when the knee later bends, so evaluate anticipated movement before finishing.

Source: https://rope365.com/sitting/

Floor positions range from relaxed cross-legged meditation to kneeling/seiza, hero pose with feet beside hips, side-sitting with ankles together, and pigeon/swan with one leg forward and the other drawn back. A cushion or block can make kneeling/hero accessible. Combine upper- and lower-body structures only after the unsupported pose is comfortable, and account for balance while the tyer moves around a person who cannot reposition freely.

Source: https://rope365.com/restrictions/

Whole-body restriction can pull opposite legs in a “running person,” cross one leg over another in a windmill pin, fasten straight arms to hips, join arms to knees, bend the body into S-curves, compact it into a ball/package, or fold it into a shrimp/ebi. For the shrimp, use a chest structure instead of a neck line and plan fast release because forward fold limits breathing. Standing S-shapes need a catcher. The central exercise is to identify which joint or line actually prevents motion and remove unnecessary pressure elsewhere.

Source: https://rope365.com/exposed/

Exposure is a negotiated effect, not an automatic property of a pose. A crab joins arms and legs on each side; open-knee/M/moon forms hold knees apart; a tuck joins wrists behind knees; a beast tie joins all four limbs forward; legs-to-chest and side-opening variants expose lower body; and a butt-up/momo form folds wrists between ankles and fixes the waist. Change orientation—back, side, belly, seated—to discover new pressure before adding sexual activity.

Source: https://rope365.com/body-challenges/

Physical challenge can emphasize forward fold, backbend, arm stretch, splits, spinal torsion, whole-body mummification, or endurance. Warm up, increase range gradually, distinguish productive exertion from joint/nerve pain, and make every endurance tie quickly releasable. Mummification adds heat, panic, circulation, breathing, and lengthy-exit hazards; incorporate several independent release points and keep a cutter ready.

text:
### Small extremities

Source: https://rope365.com/feet-and-toes/

Shoes alter ankle mechanics and may offer attachment points; a foot harness spreads pulling over the foot; soles provide tickle and pressure-point play; feet can be joined side-by-side or sole-to-sole; lines between toes add intense local contact; individual toes can be pulled; and ankle/toe lines can point or flex the foot. Small bones, nerves, and easily escaped digits demand thin-force restraint, frequent checks, and low tension. A “gravity boot” is described as useful in floorwork even though similar structures later appear in suspension.

Source: https://rope365.com/hands-and-fingers/

Hands may be paired palms-in or out, held in fists, pinned open, joined by selected fingers, placed in a wrist lock, interlaced, or sculpted. Historical open/closed-hand symbolism is context, not a safety rule. Finger lines and backward wrist pulls generate large joint leverage from little force; proceed slowly, keep knots off vulnerable digital nerves, and remember that even a small hand injury can disrupt daily life.

text:
## Nerve and circulation problems

Original URL: https://rope-topia.com/nerve-and-circulation-problems/

Archived capture: https://web.archive.org/web/20231210233011id_/http://rope-topia.com/nerve-and-circulation-problems/

The page explicitly says its author is not a medical professional and recommends medical advice for an injury. It names position, pressure, and duration as three contributors to nerve problems and urges immediate communication and action rather than waiting to identify the exact cause.

It describes localized tingling or numbness in part of a hand as a possible nerve warning and recommends untying rather than continuing. Its simplified hand map associates different finger regions with radial, median, and ulnar pathways, while acknowledging that front and back patterns vary. Such a map cannot diagnose an injury.

The article contrasts circulation sensations that tend to build gradually with nerve sensations that may appear suddenly. It cautions that circulation symptoms can mask nerve symptoms. It suggests checking sensation with light touch and checking motor control through agreed squeeze or push signals, while noting that not everyone experiences tingling. Circulation changes still require response even if the article treats sudden nerve symptoms as more urgent.

text:
### Methodology

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/methodology/

#### 01 What the lineage view shows

Fact — The page presents a public research view of shibari / kinbaku people, schools, studios, rope bars, venues, festivals, publications, platforms, material infrastructure and historical context. It is not a complete directory, a ranking, a certification registry, or a claim that every important person is visible.

Fact — Entries are added when they help explain transmission, scene infrastructure or historical context:

- Transmission — People with public rope identities — teachers, performers, organizers, authors, photographers, riggers, models, rope makers or lineage carriers — when that identity also has a graph-safe relationship or a clearly structural / historical role
- Fact — Recurring studios, dojos, festivals, conventions, communities and education platforms
- Fact — Publications, films, production labels and material projects when they shaped rope culture or connect multiple existing entries
- Fact — Historical or regional context when it explains why clusters moved or changed
Transmission — The view is intentionally selective. A person or event may be important locally and still stay out of the visible lineage if the public references are too thin, if inclusion would expose private information, or if the public evidence does not yet explain a wider historical or structural role. A well-sourced current-person profile is not enough by itself; without a reference-supported relationship, infrastructure role or historical bridge, it stays source-only / watch rather than becoming an isolated visible node.

#### 02 Evidence levels

Fact — The project separates evidence into three practical levels. What a source can support depends on which level it sits at.

##### Strong

Fact — Official bios, studio pages, event rosters, publisher pages, interviews, archived festival pages, books, public talks, or direct pages maintained by the person or organization. Supports entries, biography facts, source links and visible relationships.

##### Medium

Fact — Public social posts, directory rows, event mirrors, podcast pages, third-party writeups, or archived traces. Supports limited role, event or link context when it corroborates an already public identity.

##### Weak

Fact — Unsourced records, reposts, dead pages without archive context, search snippets, anonymous claims, private screenshots, memory or rumor. Used as a research lead, not as a public claim.

Fact — If two sources conflict, the project prefers the more direct source: the person's own current public statement, then official studio / event records, then interviews and specialist archives, then broader secondary sources. Older sources are kept only when they explain historical context and do not conflict with a subsequent correction.

#### 03 Source handling

Fact — Preferred sources are official pages, event rosters, studio biographies, interviews, archived festival pages, publisher pages, public talks and stable community documentation. Directory rows and social posts are used as leads, not as enough evidence on their own, unless they corroborate an already public role.

Fact — Each visible entry should have at least one public link, unless the entry is broad historical context supported by the public source list. Repeated sources are kept in a shared registry so readers can check the evidence without the same link being repeated in every paragraph.

Fact — Source links support verification and attribution. They do not mean the linked source endorses Shibari Atlas, and they do not import the linked text into the project's license. For visible relationships, source records must support the relationship itself — a source that only proves two entries exist, or only places them in the same broad scene, is not enough.

#### 04 Documented corpus profile

Fact — These counts describe the public material currently documented by Shibari Atlas. They do not measure the size, importance or geographic distribution of the underlying rope scene.

Fact — The profile is a release snapshot, not a score. A high count can reflect accessible archives, language access or concentrated research effort; a low count can reflect missing metadata or documentation gaps. Unknown values stay visible instead of being inferred.

- Cited entries: Fact — 1,421
