Fact — Type and language use source records as their denominator. Era and country use unique source-to-entry citations, so one source cited by two entries contributes twice. Duplicate citations on one entry contribute once. Only citations attached to people and organizations participate; citations attached to individual relationships, works or programme sections remain separate.

Fact — Open the public references catalogue to inspect individual public references. The catalogue keeps its own pagination and search; this profile publishes aggregates only.

- Linked evidence or record: [Open the public Sources catalogue](https://shibariatlas.org/sources/)
#### 05 From claim to visible map

Fact — Not every true or interesting fact becomes a visible relationship. Every claim lands at the strictest level its public evidence supports — most never reach the map:

##### Not used

Fact — An attractive inference that is misleading, unsafe, duplicate or unsupported.

##### Research lead

Fact — Worth revisiting later, but not presented publicly yet.

##### Biography context

Fact — Useful for understanding an entry but too narrow, private, ambiguous or weak to draw.

##### Visible relationship

Transmission — A source directly supports a specific connection that explains transmission, collaboration, publication, teaching or lineage.

##### Visible entry

Fact — Explains several relationships or a structurally important bridge.

Fact — The project avoids hub inflation. A magazine, festival, podcast, adult-media studio, social platform or directory is not connected to everyone it mentions. Relationships are shown only when meaningful for the lineage view — not merely because two names appear on the same page.

Fact — The project also avoids person-node inflation. Public social profiles, link hubs, interviews, directories and photo credits can be valuable source context, but if they do not support a graph-safe relationship or structural role, they remain source-only / watch. The map should not contain weak standalone current-person nodes simply because those people have real public rope identities.

#### 06 Era placement

Fact — Each visible entry is assigned to an era as a start-era label — the earliest reference-supported public period in which that subject becomes relevant to rope history in the form shown on the map.

- Fact — 1800s
- Fact — Edo / Meiji
- Fact — 1900s
- Fact — Taishō foundations
- Fact — 1950s
- Fact — Magazine era
- Fact — 1970s
- Fact — SM revolution
- Fact — 1980s
- Fact — Schools form
- Fact — 1990s
- Fact — Second generation
- Fact — 2000s
- Fact — Global diffusion
- Fact — 2010s
- Fact — Networked pedagogy
- Fact — 2020s
- Fact — Distributed infrastructure
- Fact — A person is placed by the first documented public rope role — publication, performance, teaching, organizing, named studio role, or explicit entry into the public scene.
- Fact — A studio, bar, venue, festival, platform or publication is placed by its first documented opening, founding, edition, issue or release.
- Fact — The project does not place entries by birth year, by current fame, or by whatever decade they are most active in today.
- Transmission — Later activity does not move an older entry into a newer era. If a senior practitioner later studies with a younger teacher, that is recorded as subsequent cross-era study; their era still reflects their own public starting period.
Fact — When one continuous node would collapse distinct historical phases into a misleading single band, the project splits that subject into separate era entries instead of dragging the whole history into the present.

#### 07 Relationship categories

Fact — The view distinguishes relationship strength with a small set of public categories. Each has a categorically different line so it survives even when the map repaints edges by school colour.

##### Deshi

Transmission — Explicit formal apprenticeship, named successor, or sustained primary-teacher lineage. The only solid line.

##### Certification

Fact — Instructor license, school authorization or formal rank that is public — but not framed as apprenticeship. Shown as a double rail.

##### Influence

Transmission — Workshop study, private lessons, named stylistic influence or non-formal learning. The lightest tie.

##### Founded / operates

Fact — Established, owns, operates, hosts or anchors a structured school, studio, platform, event or media line.

##### Appeared / credited

Fact — Verified presenter, workshop, festival, stage, magazine, film, production or other credited appearance.

##### Collaboration

Fact — Co-created, co-organized, partner project, staff/editor role or durable rope partnership framed as real working ties.

Fact — Workshop attendance alone is not treated as formal apprenticeship. Event co-presence never creates a person-to-person collaboration edge by itself. To avoid hub inflation, broad teacher records fan out only to teachers who already have a node or are independently graph-worthy.

##### How Trace Connection chooses a path

Transmission — Transmission uses only person-to-person apprenticeship, certification and explicit study / style / mixed influence. All documented connections can also use collaborations, studios, events, media and context. The route is calculated from the complete public graph, so saved visual filters never change a shared trace URL.

Fact — The algorithm prefers direct transmission semantics and adds a kind-and-degree penalty to intermediate entity hubs such as festivals or broad context entries. Equal routes prefer fewer steps and then immutable relationship IDs. Source counts, confidence and editorial assessments are not route weights. Every step keeps its recorded direction, traversal direction, public role / year / note and evidence; absent facts are shown as not recorded rather than inferred.

#### 08 Living people & privacy

Fact — For living or contemporary people, the project uses public scene names by default. Birth years, legal names, private residences and adult-media aliases are not added unless the person uses them in a stable public professional context and the information is necessary for the public presentation.

Fact — Missing birth years are expected. They are not a data-quality failure. Locations are public scene anchors — a studio city, festival city, teaching base or country-level scene context — not private addresses.

Safety / consent — If a living person asks for a correction, removal, identity split, privacy downgrade or safer wording, that request is handled as a high-priority signal. Public safety and consent matter more than preserving a visually dense display.

#### 09 Queer, gender & sexuality context

Fact — Queer, trans, nonbinary, women / femininities or marginalized-community context may be included when it is part of the public rope-facing identity, event scope, community mission, pedagogy or historical role being documented.

Fact — For people, use only self-description, a direct public source maintained by the person or their project, or an official event / studio / publisher bio. Keep the wording close to the source. Do not broaden a specific public statement into a larger identity label, and do not infer identity from participation in a queer event, partnerships, appearance or scene reputation.

Fact — The graph keeps no dedicated identity field or "representatives" taxonomy. Identity context belongs in biography / source context, and should never be used to rank, tokenize, out, deanonymize or expose sensitive identity beyond the source's own public framing.

#### 10 Self-reported information

Fact — Direct corrections or additions from the person an entry characterizes may be used when they clearly allow publication — by email, direct message, public comment or another first-person channel. Self-reported information is treated as first-party correction material, not as a public source; private emails, messages and screenshots are not published in the source list.

Transmission — Allowed self-reported updates include preferred name, spelling, pronouns, role, location scope, public links or removal requests; corrections to biography, teacher framing, partner context, studio affiliation or event framing; identity splits where two public names were incorrectly merged; and privacy downgrades. For strong lineage, rank or controversial claims, a public source is preferred.

#### 11 Adult-media & sensitive sources

Fact — Adult-media sources are handled narrowly. They are included only when they support a rope-specific public role: rigger, bondage director, rope consultant, production rope educator, selected rope-video work, or a production project that materially shaped rope visual culture.

Fact — The project does not import filmographies, one-off performer credits, scraped adult databases, ambiguous aliases or links that expose private identity beyond a public rope role. Sensitive sources should never be used to out, deanonymize, shame, rank or sensationalize a person. If a fact is not needed to explain rope transmission or infrastructure, it stays out.

#### 12 Licensing & attribution

Fact — The public dataset is split by content type. Its structured facts can be released under CC0 where legally possible: public entry identifiers, names, aliases, dates, scene locations, entry categories and relationship categories. Any public export must exclude biographies and other expressive prose, contact and reference URLs, canonical source identifiers, source-registry metadata and internal editorial fields. Where a fact has public evidence, an export may carry only an opaque citation key that can be resolved via this site's public citation layer.

Fact — Third-party source material remains under its original license. Biographies and notes should be independently written factual summaries — the project does not intentionally import verbatim third-party prose or close translations. If copied prose is found, it is rewritten, removed, or attributed and licensed according to the source.

#### 13 Editorial checks

Fact — Each update is checked against the same editorial principles:

- Fact — Public links or source references for updated entries and relationships
- Fact — Conservative relationship categories with short explanatory notes
- Fact — Weak leads held back until better public evidence appears
- Fact — Well-sourced but isolated current-person leads kept source-only instead of becoming weak standalone nodes
- Fact — Tempting but unsupported inferences left out
- Fact — Consistency checks for relationship categories, dates, locations and public links
Fact — Known uncertainty is preferable to false precision. The project should say less when the sources say less.

#### 14 Corrections

Fact — If the correction comes directly from the person an entry characterizes, a public source is preferred but not always required; the person should state explicitly what may be published. Preferred outcomes are precise: fix a bio sentence, downgrade apprenticeship to influence, remove an unsupported relationship, add a source link, split two merged identities, or hide a private detail.

Fact — A correction, removal, access or privacy request about a record describing you can be sent via the contribution contact. Mark that the request concerns you and state what may be published. The message is reviewed manually; it is not published as a source and the project does not promise a fixed outcome or response period.

Fact — Help build the atlas

Fact — Corrections, missing sources and requests about a record describing you use the contribution guide.

rights_basis.basis: The artifact is an original compressed digest and states that external reference pages were not copied, but the legacy corpus manifest records no explicit reuse license for the site's own text.
limitations: External source-catalogue metadata does not license the referenced works.
Do not redistribute site prose, images, graph assets, or external reference bodies from this registry record.
The registry inventories the declared artifact role and does not provide a legal opinion.

text:
### Updates

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/updates/

#### Format

Fact — The page presents a project changelog, not a news feed — it covers changes to this dataset and this interface, not the wider shibari/kinbaku scene. Each entry groups a period of work into three kinds of change: Dataset (people, entities and connections added or corrected), Research (naming, lineage or classification calls revised), and Interface (features and fixes to the site itself).

Fact — Each entry groups completed changes by outcome and reflects the public atlas at the end of the week, omitting temporary work that did not remain part of the release. For the editorial rules behind the dataset, see methodology; for the public references behind it, see sources.

- Linked evidence or record: [methodology](https://shibariatlas.org/methodology/); [sources](https://shibariatlas.org/sources/)
#### Log

##### Evidence-aware profiles and the 0.2 release checkpoint

Transmission — Closed the 0.2 release checkpoint around exact evidence, resilient delivery and mobile Lineage behavior, while extending the historical event archive and regional atlas coverage.

- Transmission — Research Expanded exact claim-level provenance for selected life dates, active periods, institutional histories and formal lineage fields, with structured source limitations and regression checks guarding citation metadata.
- Transmission — Interface Connected reviewed citations to selected profile facts and relationship evidence, added readable static Lineage and profile fallbacks for missing JavaScript or bundles, and simplified profile, citation and correction controls.
- Transmission — Interface Shipped a mobile-first Lineage directory that opens profiles in Hyper Focus, then tightened camera fitting, touch behavior, edge routing, deep-link centering and cross-surface links between Lineage, Map and Events.
- Transmission — Interface Added production-preview smoke checks, a Node compatibility lane, retained browser failure artifacts, bounded Lineage and Map delivery to 128 shards, reduced Sources page size and introduced release-blocking capacity budgets.
- Fact — Dataset Extended the event archive spanning 2004–2023 and added or enriched people, studios and communities such as Kokoro Kinbaku, Fittglitter, Turo Shibari and Shibariland, Kanso, Van Darkholme, Lochai, Rope Jam Studio, Shibari Muses, Shibari Study Valencia and Entwine NV.
##### Festival archive and a steadier Lineage

Fact — Expanded the historical events ledger and archive-roster links while hardening Hyper Focus for iPhone memory limits, touch navigation and dense relationship views.

- Fact — Dataset Backfilled occurrences spanning BoundCon, EURIX, RopeFest, LFRA, Onawa and Omuchi Asobi, Rope Spirit, Helsinki Shibari, Bondage Expo Dallas, Shi Fest Ukraine, Rome Bondage Week, La Nuit des Cordes, BOUND London, Bound In Boston, ROPECRAFT, Prague Shibari Festival, MoscowKnot and RopeDay.
- Fact — Research Connected verified archive participants to existing profiles spanning ROPECRAFT, Helsinki, Shi Fest, Rope Spirit, BOUND London, EURIX, Onawa and Omuchi, Prague and Rome, without turning simple co-presence into lineage claims.
- Transmission — Interface Replaced oversized Lineage canvas layers with viewport-sized iPhone overlays, released hidden-tab canvas memory, improved dense note-label routing and edge highlighting, and tightened mobile Hyper Focus selection and side-panel behavior.
- Fact — Dataset Applied the Kinbaku Today article-family audit and broadened reference-supported coverage via additions such as the Rope Podcast hosts, Knots N’Roots Costa Rica, Triangle Shibari, Tüdeltreff Kiel and Dov / Washi Nawashi.
##### Hyper Focus and a broader contextual atlas

Transmission — Introduced a shareable one-hop Lineage view, reorganized recent-era and regional context layers, and expanded connected profiles and public contact coverage via focused source audits.

- Transmission — Interface Added Hyper Focus as a shareable one-hop Lineage view with category lanes, era rows, always-visible connection notes, mobile access, touch-friendly controls and curated first-visit framing.
- Fact — Dataset Split recent historical context into clearer 2010s and 2020s layers, added explicit regional scene sublayers and era spans, and surfaced those spans on selected context profiles.
- Fact — Dataset Expanded connected coverage spanning Japanese studios and practitioners, festival performers and regional communities, such as Maitake, Toronawa / MilkyWay, Enbushinbakukai, Hanikamu Nawakai, Enchou Studio, Why Knot Lille and Thailand BDSM Community.
- Fact — Research Completed broad public-contact and source-family passes spanning Bluesky, Instagram, Shibarimap, books and Kinbaku Today, while leaving weak or unstable leads out until stronger evidence appears.
##### Faster Lineage delivery and a global infrastructure pass

Transmission — Made the Lineage and Map lighter, faster and more accessible, expanded local-scene and event coverage worldwide, and completed a systematic reconciliation of relationships, entity types and source links.

- Transmission — Interface Moved the Lineage graph into a cached static payload and made biographies load only when opened, dramatically shrinking profile-page HTML; also sped up zooming and path highlighting, reduced mobile edge rendering and fixed narrow-screen edge clipping and bottom-sheet gestures.
- Fact — Interface Reduced repeated work spanning the Map, minimap and home search, added keyboard and screen-reader access with visible focus treatment, improved event-page readability and corrected Event and Lineage structured-data warnings.
- Fact — Dataset Added people, studios, communities and venues spanning Perth, Singapore, Montenegro, Latin America, Cape Town, continental Europe and North America, alongside new occurrences for Alive Kinbaku Festival, Men Roping Men, BoFeWo and Kinbaku Dreams.
- Transmission — Research Reviewed sparse relationship records, major event and studio hubs, era placement and entity classification; synchronized verified event appearances with the Lineage and introduced clearer venue and event subtypes.
- Fact — Research Reconciled duplicate studios, shared URLs and project-versus-person link scope spanning a broad source pass, correcting many ambiguous profile, team, event and institutional references without inflating the graph.
##### A new map and connected reference pages

Fact — Rebuilt the geographic atlas and its public reference pages as clearer, searchable surfaces, while expanding global profiles, event coverage and historical source context.

- Fact — Interface Redesigned the Map around a dark labelled basemap, category-coloured pins and clusters, a collapsible country-grouped index and a draggable mobile bottom sheet; it now defaults to living, currently active people and places and groups country-level records under shared pins.
- Fact — Interface Rebuilt About, Methodology and Sources as a connected reference set with live dataset counts, evidence-level explainers, an anchored FAQ and a searchable, filterable source index that exposes previously hidden catalog and database records.
- Fact — Interface Added dedicated pages for individual event occurrences with structured event data, introduced a distinct visual treatment for certification relationships, and fixed antimeridian centering, popup auto-pan and untimed programme-row layout.
- Fact — Dataset Expanded people, studios, communities and event links spanning Japan, Europe, Asia-Pacific and Latin America, such as new programme and roster context for Shibari Camp Poland, Prague Autumn, QuIRKS, Kinky Breizh Ropecamp and Querverbindungen.
- Fact — Research Deepened historical provenance via focused Nawa-art, Kinbakunomicon, Kitan Club and related media-source audits, and separated instructor certification from broader teaching and influence relationships.
##### Timeline Ledger and global scene coverage

Fact — Rebuilt annual event browsing into a clearer Timeline Ledger, expanded profiles and rosters spanning Japan, Europe and the wider global scene, and strengthened the atlas's public status and evidence workflow.

- Fact — Interface Redesigned yearly Events as a month-by-month Timeline Ledger with a current-date divider, status badges, compact rosters, linked profile names and clear event-site and Lineage actions.
- Fact — Interface Added a site-wide Beta Status badge, contact blocks for studios and events, bestowed-name profile metadata and structured-data fixes for About, Methodology and Sources.
- Fact — Dataset Expanded coverage spanning Latin America, South Africa, the Baltics, Belfast, Hong Kong, New Zealand, India and Japan, such as an extensive Ichinawakai instructor pass and new studio and community clusters.
- Fact — Research Filled 2026 schedule and roster gaps for Bogotá, EURIX, Prague, Pinse, BoundCon, Shibari Camp Poland, Danish Shibari Festival, Queerope and Onawa Asobi Europe, and added Kinbaku Yuga, RopeFest and International Shibari Film Festival coverage.
- Transmission — Research Simplified relationship types while separating appearances and editorial context, then added a generated source ledger and event-lineage cross-check to keep evidence and event-derived links auditable.
##### Post-launch expansion and relationship provenance

Transmission — Expanded documented people, places and events spanning several regions, completed a major relationship-provenance pass, and made atlas navigation and Lineage reading more precise after launch.

- Fact — Dataset Expanded the atlas with new clusters spanning Japan, China, Korea, the Balkans, Central Europe, Canada, Australia and Latin America, alongside selected studios, dojos, festivals and documentary-film entries.
- Transmission — Research Completed a source pass spanning performance, collaboration, dojo, operator and study or influence relationships, adding role and evidence metadata, source references, chronology checks and explicit influence kinds.
- Fact — Research Audited Nawapedia's kinbakushi and model categories, a Japanese historical chronology and several podcast and source families, promoting well-supported profiles while keeping weak leads out of the atlas.
- Fact — Interface Added a Places catalogue and direct city or country map focus, event-occurrence deep links, accent-insensitive search and explicit profile contact links.
- Fact — Interface Improved mobile selection framing and zoom text sharpness, marked recently added profiles, and clarified parallel relationship paths, labels and endpoints.
##### Shibari Atlas public beta launch

Chronology — After a week of launch preparation, the project — until then called Bakushi Map — relaunched publicly as Shibari Atlas for the first time: a live site on its own domain with search-engine visibility, mobile support, and a published data methodology behind it.

- Transmission — Interface Renamed the project from Bakushi Map to Shibari Atlas, a broader public umbrella covering the map and events alongside the lineage, and opened it to the public at bakushimap.org, with per-entity profile pages, a sitemap, a custom 404 page, and social-preview images generated from the live map; added an About overlay that opens automatically on first visit alongside dedicated Methodology and Sources pages explaining the project's inclusion criteria and sourcing standards; and built out mobile support with pinch-zoom, touch panning, and a collapsible filter bar with a bottom-sheet detail panel.
- Fact — Dataset Substantially expanded regional coverage — Russian, Ukrainian, Belarusian, Polish and other national scenes, such as diaspora connections following the war in Ukraine — and added many new festival and event nodes worldwide, plus new studios, podcasts and community platforms as tracked sources, alongside a new authored-works category linking writers to their published books.
- Fact — Research Published a public data methodology covering inclusion criteria, source standards, and a narrow policy for adult-media sources tied to a specific rope-related professional role; reclassified roughly 40 entries from a generic "venue" type into distinct event and studio categories and gave adult-media production companies their own category separate from teaching studios; and established a policy for handling self-reported corrections from people who appear in the dataset.

### Rope Material Globalization

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/contexts/rope-material-globalization/

Fact — Rope Material Globalization, 2000s-2020s, is a context in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2000s-2020s

Fact — Era: 2000s

Fact — Historical transition context for the global supply bridge that made shibari-specific rope more portable spanning scenes: early handmade Western rope businesses, Japanese asanawa suppliers, European craft / supplier routes and the first broadly visible stockist layer. The mature 2020s JBO-free, studio-shop and distributor ecosystem is documented separately as Material & Supply Infrastructure.

### Safety / Consent / Rope-Bottom Pedagogy

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/contexts/safety-consent-rope-bottom-pedagogy/

Safety / consent — Safety / Consent / Rope-Bottom Pedagogy, 2010s-2020s, is a context in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2010s-2020s

Fact — Era: 2010s

Safety / consent — 2010s context for rope education becoming explicitly structured around risk awareness, consent, negotiation, body mechanics and rope-bottom agency. Crash Restraint, Rope Study, Rope Connections, Evie Vane's rope-bottoming work, Rope Podcast / Friction Live body-mechanics material, Studio Kokoro's rope-bottom resources and later ropehelp.ch mark this as a pedagogy layer rather than a lineage.

Fact — Active: 2020s–

Fact — Era: 2020s

Safety / consent — Veneto branch of Shibari Dojo Italy. The official I Dojo page records Veneto Shibari Dojo as a multilevel lesson hub with multiple instructors, a safety video course, kit-purchase option and lessons in Campodarsego. Current BDSM Shop lesson pages for May 24, 2025 and June 20, 2026 both still route the branch via Via Antoniana 218/B, while a public Billetto `Peer Rope Veneto Shibari Dojo` listing names the same host shell as `Niō Dojo - Locale privato`. Current Shibari Dojo / MaestroBD posts name Franky Ropes as the Veneto branch instructor referent and June lesson / practice coordinator. The atlas keeps the Veneto branch as a city-level school entry and models Niō Dojo separately as the current host space rather than collapsing the branch into a venue claim.

### Austin Ropecraft Symposium

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/events/austin-ropecraft-symposium/

Fact — Austin Ropecraft Symposium, 2005–2006, is documented as an American event in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2005–2006

Fact — Era: 2000s

Fact — Historical rope-education symposium held in Austin in 2005 and 2006. The inaugural December 2005 edition described itself as unaffiliated and not-for-profit, sold out and brought together an international teaching roster; Austin Ropecraft Symposium 2 followed on September 1-3, 2006. Public programmes connect the symposium to Bridgett / Lee Harrington, Lochai, Mark of DV8House, Lew Rubens and Graydancer. This two-edition symposium is distinct from Graydancer's later ROPECRAFT convention brand launched in 2016.

#### Reference links

- [2005 retrospective](https://web.archive.org/web/20060901003837/http://www.ropecraft.info/ARS_2005.html)

Fact — Active: 2014–

Fact — Era: 2010s

Fact — Montreal annual rope-bondage performance event established by Ebi McKnotty and Dunter in 2014, inspired by Morpheous' Bondage Extravaganza and tied to Nuit Blanche / Montréal en Lumière. The official current site says NDC has been part of Nuit Blanche every year since, with the 2021 edition virtual and a 2024 return in person at the usual Espace des Arts. Public listings and archives place the 2015, 2016, 2017 and 2019 editions at Espace des Arts / 9 Sainte-Catherine Est; the 2021 page frames the event as a seven-hour digital edition with 100+ artists, simultaneous performances, circus, erotic bondage, acrobatic and decorative rope, and photography exhibition context.

Fact — Karada House's queer rope conference for LGBTQIA+ people and womên, focused mostly on shibari, kinbaku and Japanese-inspired bondage. Queerope exists in both virtual and in-person Berlin formats: the February 11-16 2026 virtual edition published online workshops, performances, demonstrations and discussions, while Queerope Berlin 2026 is a five-day in-person conference at KARA / Karada House on September 2-6 with 37 facilitated spaces / workshops. Published 2026 line-ups include Caritia, Ceci Ferox, Mouse, Murasaki Haruan, Naoko, Renée de Sans and Sophia Rose among facilitators; the Berlin edition also records Juliette & Olive, Lief Bound, Icarus / Icky, Nana Quinn, Thumper and Bondagious. text:
### Kinbaku Society of Berlin

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/magazines/kinbaku-society-berlin/

Fact — Kinbaku Society of Berlin, 2019–, is documented as a German magazine in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2019–

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — Berlin-centered kinbaku research forum and magazine project associated with Discover Kinbaku and Die Goldbomben. The official KSB site says the project began as a forum for studying kinbaku and the erotic / aesthetic ideas around it, then grew into a printed magazine with photography, poetry, fiction, essays, documentation, drawings and interviews. Discover Kinbaku characterizes the society as a forum with no formal membership, currently expressed via the KSB Magazine and Kinbaku Salon, and its magazine page frames KSB as a twice-yearly publication open to submissions and focused on Japanese underground culture, rope bondage, adult entertainment, aesthetics and Western reception. Nawapedia records the magazine as member-produced from December 2019 and rooted in a Studio 6x6 circle; Indiecon records it as an independent publishing project by international artists researching Japanese rope bondage and related subcultures. The KSB contributor page records Swen Brandy / Carnivore among contributors, and the official issue #4 page credits Carnivore for design. Die Goldbomben's current preorder page frames KSB Magazine N° I-26 as a 500-copy hand-numbered edition planned for the end of summer 2026, with original photography by Sugiura Norio.

### Roca Magazine

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/magazines/roca-magazine/

Fact — Roca Magazine, 2021–, is documented as a Japanese magazine in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2021–

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — Tokyo-centered contemporary kinbaku print magazine edited and published by Kitaro Kasukabe via Studio Roca. Studio Roca's live shop says Kinbaku Magazine Roca began in 2021 and has expanded to five volumes with readers around the world; Kasukabe's 2024 Roca no Kai note frames the magazine as preserving and advancing kinbaku culture, supporting community growth and expanding from his photography into essays, columns, other artists' works and illustrations. The Roca #3 shop record characterizes the magazine as dedicated to disseminating kinbaku culture in Japan and worldwide, with contents such as Naka Akira / Shizuki Iroha / Sato Erisa photography and a Yugen Studio Magazine collaboration. The Roca #5 product page records a July 2025, 148-page bilingual issue with a special feature on Erisa Satoh, Yukiscream & Miya content and a Roca 2025 exhibition report.

### Ogawa Ropes

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/materials/ogawa-ropes/

Fact — Ogawa Ropes, 1929-; shibari-supply trace 2020s-, is documented as a Japanese material in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 1929-; shibari-supply trace 2020s-

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — Tokyo rope shop / supplier alternate public name: 小川ロープ. Ogawa's official site frames it as a jute-rope and industrial-rope specialist established in 1929, not as a shibari-only brand. In shibari material infrastructure, Ogawa matters because current rope shops and education spaces sell, prepare, use or teach with Japan-sourced Ogawa jute for kinbaku / shibari use: NekoNeko sells untreated 6 mm Ogawa jute rope directly at its Belgrade studio, Shibari Study sells Berlin-prepared Ogawa Tossa jute via its shop extension, Anatomie / Shibari Store sells natural and dyed Ogawa variants as part of its studio feedback loop, and RopeMarks names Ogawa as one of the Japanese jute ropes he uses and stocks. It has direct studio / platform / practitioner stockist connections, not as a school or lineage entry.

Works and teaches internationally with Barkas; together they established TheSpace2, Vancouver's first dedicated rope space, now continued as Studio Allegory, and teach a process-focused style centered on interaction, emotion and the person in ropes. text:
### Alexander MA

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/alexander-ma/

Fact — Alexander MA is documented as a German rigger in the Naka-ryū.

Fact — Era: 2010s

Transmission — RIGGER · TEACHER

Fact — Berlin-centered kinbaku rigger, researcher, educator and performer behind Discover Kinbaku with Natasha NawaTaNeko. Discover Kinbaku says Alexander and Natasha have tied together since becoming a couple in 2011, offer private classes / coaching in Berlin and international workshops, and established the Kinbaku Society and its Berlin magazine. Earlier public pages under the NawaRonin name place Alexander in the Warsaw / Poland teaching scene, where his official NawaRonin about page says he established Shibari Dojo Warsaw, operated it from 2015 to 2019 and resumed education work from 2023 after the Covid break. IKSK characterizes Alexander Madl as a researcher and kinbaku artist teaching traditional Japanese rope bondage in Berlin and spanning Europe. Alexander's official profile traces his rope turning point to New Year's Day 2008, then names a 2015 introduction to Naka-style semenawa via Scot Kinbaku and Namarié and intensive 2016-2021 study with Riccardo Wildties. Current Discover Kinbaku pages present his Elements System as a staged didactic framework for riggers, combining technique, philosophy, aesthetics, embodiment, movement, communication, limits and consent from first steps to advanced creative expression. Discover Kinbaku frames their work as rooted in semenawa by Akira Naka, with encounters with Naka Akira, Sugiura Norio and Miho Ikeda as key influences; Alexander's Bodies under Pressure concept is a semenawa / somatic-body approach formalized via workshops and a 2025 essay. Public Studio Kokoro listings for Discover Kinbaku use the name Alexander NawaRonin, which is treated here as an alias of the same Berlin / Discover Kinbaku figure rather than a separate person entry. Prague Shibari Festival Spring 2026 records Alexander MA and Natasha NawaTaNeko as presenters / performers.

Fact — School: Naka-ryū

### Dany Cox

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/dany-cox/

Fact — Dany Cox is an Australian rope artist.

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — ROPE ARTIST · PERFORMER

Fact — Perth-centered Australian rope artist and performer using the public identity Dany Cox / `@danydayze`. X-Press Magazine names Cox as a Perth shibari artist returning to Fringe World in 2024 with `Tethered`, after earlier `Theatre of Bondage` and `Bound to You` performances, and its 2025 review names Miiko Rose as Dany's performance partner for a later `Tethered` staging. Humanitix's `Melt - A Shibari Ritual` host and event pages describe Dany as a Perth-centered rope artist blending performance, ritual and relational awareness and frame Dany / Miiko as a duo performing and creating together. The Bindery's 2024 Humanitix workshop listings separately bill Dany Dayze as demo top for Himboy's Rope Communication 101 / 102 workshops, while Shibari Adelaide's 2024 listing bills `@Danydayze` and `@Annakidd__` as interstate performers for a night of shibari stage performances. The current Linktree preserves 2026 travel / booking context for shoots, lessons, sessions, performances and workshops, routes upcoming workshops via On Sacral Ground, and records Perth rope resources such as Ignition Perth; a public Ignition recap separately names `@danydayze` among the people who lit up its Down the Rabbit Hole event. Fox Lab Fine Art independently frames Dany as a visual artist whose shibari work is available as fine-art prints.

### Akechi Denki (明智伝鬼)

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/denki-akechi/

Transmission — Akechi Denki (明智伝鬼), 1940–2005, is documented as a Japanese nawashi in the Akechi lineage.

Chronology — Born: 1940

Chronology — Died: 2005

Fact — Era: 1980s

Transmission — NAWASHI · TEACHER

Transmission — Central figure of the late-Shōwa / early-Heisei kinbaku boom and founder of the Akechi-ryū lineage. In 1978 met Sakurada Denjirō and Shin Sakurada at GSG Planning; in 1985 took over Sakurada's stage shows. Opened Studio Phantom in Shibuya in 1987 for photo sessions and SM experimental kinbaku theatre. Established Bakuyukai with Takumi Miura in 1996. Public evidence supports Akechi Kanna as a direct student / Akechi-style successor context, while Saikatsu / Kazami accounts identify Raika, Enka and Kadera as Akechi's formal women students, while Saikatsu / Kazami accounts frame Saikatsu as assistant and friend rather than deshi. His work shaped later Akechi-style transmission via Kanna-linked teachers such as Ren Yagami and influenced Hajime Kinoko's early bar-period workshop exposure.

Transmission — School: Akechi lineage

### Dirty Kudo (ダーティ工藤)

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/dirty-kudo/

Fact — Dirty Kudo (ダーティ工藤), born 1954, is documented as a Japanese kinbakushi.

Chronology — Born: 1954

Fact — Era: 1990s

Fact — KINBAKUSHI · DIRECTOR

Fact — Japanese kinbakushi, director, producer and writer born in Hokkaido in 1954. Nawapedia says he discovered Kitan Club in 1969 and taught himself kinbaku, moved to Tokyo in 1973, wrote about SM-film history in S&M Sniper during the 1980s, directed his first title for Art Video in 1990, started the Dirty label in 1994 and DK Productions in 2000. Kinbaku Books' Ropemarks archive confirms his 1998 rope-mark photo-book and companion Jomonshiki DVD context.

### Eric Shore

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/eric-shore/

Fact — Eric Shore is a French rigger.

Fact — Era: 2010s

Transmission — RIGGER · TEACHER · ORGANIZER

Transmission — Lyon-centered French shibari / kinbaku teacher, rigger and workshop organizer. Eric Shore's official bio says he discovered shibari proper in autumn 2017, met Riccardo Wildties in 2018, continued Kinbaku LuXuria study and obtained official certification in December 2022, while still studying in that direction. The same public site frames an ongoing Lyon teaching practice via private lessons, small-group classes and guest-teacher workshops. A separate essay on the site characterizes a broader rope chronology going back to 2011 and professional-level kinbaku context from 2013; presented as biography context rather than expanded into older scene connections from current evidence alone.

### Esinem

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/esinem/

Fact — Esinem is a British nawashi in the Osada / Western.

Fact — Era: 2000s

Transmission — NAWASHI · TEACHER

Fact — British kinbaku practitioner and educator centered in London. One of the West's early adopters of Japanese rope bondage. Studied under Arisue Go, Osada Steve, Hajime Kinoko, Ranki Kazami, Akira Naka, and Yukimura Haruki. Co-organiser of BOUND (Europe's premier monthly shibari event, London) and the London Festival of the Art of Japanese Bondage. Represented the UK at Japan's first international kinbaku event, Toubaku. Collaborated with fashion designer Tom Ford (2013). Co-teaches ESINEM Shibari Classes with partner Nina Russ. Author of the first English-language kinbaku tutorial DVD series "Japanese Rope Bondage: Tying people, not parcels."

Fact — School: Osada / Western

### Glü Wür

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/gluwur/

Fact — Glü Wür is a Chilean artist.

Fact — Era: 2010s

Fact — ARTIST · PERFORMER

Fact — Chilean artist, performer and rope practitioner centered in Barcelona. Her own site characterizes a background in theatre, painting, sculpture, jewelry design, gemmology, aerial circus and shibari; she entered Barcelona's shibari community in 2013, first as a model, then via self-suspension and a circus / Japanese-bondage performance practice. Her rope-art archive records performances spanning Europe and the Americas, such as Big Bag at EURIX Berlin, Onawa Asobi Europe with Rouse Renoir, Rome Bondage Week, Shibari Freak Camp, BOUND London, Folsom Street Fair and Anatomie Studio. Sala de Luz's profile names her as Paola, says she has taught self-tying and shibari since 2016, organizes Hitchin Bitches BCN, and opened Sala de Luz in 2022 to connect arts and sexuality. EURIX XXVI spring 2025 records GLUWUR as a presenter.

text:
### Arisue Go (有末剛)

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/go-arisue/

Fact — Arisue Go (有末剛), born 1954, is documented as a Japanese nawashi.

Chronology — Born: 1954

Fact — Era: 1990s

Transmission — NAWASHI · PERFORMER · TEACHER

Transmission — Tokyo-centered kinbaku performer, teacher and author born in 1954. Nawapedia and Kinbaku Wiki place him as a senior connection figure whose early rope interest was shaped by Seiu Ito's paintings and Minomura Ko / Akira's writings, then by 1970s work for SM Select, SM Fan and SM-club shows while he was still a university student. Ohta's publisher profile also characterizes him as inheriting Ito / Minomura's conceptual line and working spanning 1970s-1980s SM magazines, Nikkatsu / Toei / V-cinema rope direction, photo books and overseas collaborations. Did the rope for the 2000s Flower and Snake remake series and the 1987 Angel Guts: Akai Nawa Hateru Made, appeared in Hiroki Ryuichi's Bakushi documentary, and became one of the early Japanese kinbakushi to teach and publish for international audiences. Selected authored works include Kinbakushi A's Ecstasy And Gloom, Arisue's Kinbaku Theory and Practices, and the 2009 Kinbaku Mind and Techniques manuals.

text:
### Kai Navarro

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/kai-navarro/

Fact — Kai Navarro is a rigger.

Fact — Era: 2010s

Transmission — RIGGER · TEACHER · ORGANIZER

Fact — Costa Rican rigger, rope educator, organizer and editor active in the EscenaCR orbit since the early 2010s. Public MsC Worldwide presenter bios identify `Master Kai` as active in community from 2012, co-director of EscenaCR and editor of its e-magazine from 2013, a shibari instructor in the rope community later known as `Sinistra`, creator of `Sinistra` as a `nawakai` in 2017, MAsT San José director from 2019 and organizer of monthly `Shibari Live Shows` from 2022; the 2026 presenter page separately records him as director at Sinistra (Nawakai). Additional public traces connect Kai Navarro / Rigger Kai to EscenaCR rope education via a 2022 Genially presentation on Japanese shibari aesthetics, older EscenaCR WordPress translation/editorial work around Yukimura material, the Rigger Kai Notion blog, and the current Aethy `Rigger Kai` profile. The Aethy profile frames him as an EscenaCR director, Latin American / MsC presenter and rope performer, while surfacing Spanish translation / lesson material, peer-rope, monthly Japanese-style shibari / kinbaku workshop and private-class context. These sources support a compact Costa Rica rope-organizer entry, but do not by themselves justify lineage, rank or teacher-study connections.

#### Reference links

- [Rigger Kai's Blog](https://ritzy-close-484.notion.site/Rigger-Kai-s-Blog-37283033892c80a3ae15ca744ae00665)
- [Aethy profile](https://aethy.com/@Riggerkai)

### Kanso

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/kanso/

Fact — Kanso is documented as an American kinbaku artist.

Fact — Era: 2010s

Transmission — KINBAKU ARTIST · TEACHER

Transmission — Bay Area kinbaku artist, performer, teacher and organizer using the public identity Kanso / Kanso Kinbaku. The Rope Circle instructor page characterizes him as a Bay Area-centered kinbaku artist, performer and sex-positive educator who encountered Japanese rope bondage during a 2006 performance in Japan and started The Rope Circle in 2017 to make shibari education more accessible. SF Station's 2019 Rope Circle class listing says he co-created Bondage Erotique in 2010, started Kiss of Rope in 2017, taught rope intensives around the United reports, performed in art / festival contexts and served as a regular SF Armory / Kink.com rope instructor for four years, teaching a year-long curriculum via suspension. Kinbaku Today's 2014 interview preserves his Bondage Erotique public identity, Blue collaboration context and interest in late-Showa / Nureki / Naka-inspired kinbaku.

Transmission — New York City-centered rope bondage artist, self-suspender, photographer, performer and teacher. Her own bio says she came to rope via demo modeling, then became primarily self-taught via trial and error, workshops and private lessons in the US and abroad. She teaches self-suspension and partnered tying via private lessons in NYC, creates rope performances and rope-focused photography, and works as a rope-bondage specialist for shoots, performances and content creation. ### Randa Mai (乱田舞)

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/mai-randa/

Fact — Randa Mai (乱田舞), 1959–2022, is documented as a Japanese nawashi.

Chronology — Born: 1959

Chronology — Died: 2022

Fact — Era: 1990s

Fact — NAWASHI · AV DIRECTOR

Fact — Japanese nawashi and adult-video director (25 March 1959 – 2022); self-described 'S&M entertainer' rather than nawashi. Alias loosely translates as 'wild dancer'. Versed in Shōrinji Kempō as a martial artist. Launched his career in the adult industry in the 1990s, collaborating with SM-themed magazines where his distinctive whip-and-discipline-driven style emerged. Created the influential 'Snake Bondage' (Jabaku / 蛇縛) series for Attackers and many instructional rope videos, and is often associated in subsequent historical writing with the iconic image of the rigger atop a gyaku-ebi tsuri suspension. In the early 2000s he became a dominant force in Tokyo's shibari scene, helping fill the void left by Akechi Denki (d. 2005). From 2016 he ran the SM bar Ranbukan, also known publicly as Bar Randa Mai, in Roppongi, and he performed and taught abroad in Europe and the United reports.

### Mark De Viate

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/mark-de-viate/

Fact — Mark De Viate is an Australian rigger.

Fact — Era: 2000s

Transmission — RIGGER · TEACHER · ORGANIZER

Fact — Sydney-centered shibari educator, performer and community organizer, publicly documented via DV8House from 2001 and Studio Kink Sydney from its 2015 formation. Studio Kink frames Mark as a co-founder and instructor, a shibari educator / performer, a 2005 Austin Ropecraft Symposium presenter and later ShibariCon educator whose rope work was refined via Osada Steve mentorship in Japan and grounded in Akechi Denki tradition context. The ShibaRicon 2015 programme gives the stronger 2006 Osada influence wording, places his rope style in Akechi Denki lineage context, notes time tying with Hajime Kinoko, Akechi Kanna and Yukimura Haruki, and records an invited 2011 Toubaku performance trace. Studio Kink's online program includes Mark's Introduction to Shibari, Fundamentals and DV8 Hishi TK courses.

Transmission — Master Set is a Russian teacher.

Fact — Era: 2010s

Transmission — TEACHER · NAWASHI · ORGANISER · PERFORMER

Transmission — Moscow bondage artist, teacher, performer and organizer in the RopeFest / ArtCo ecosystem. The ArtCo explainer names Master Set as the organizer responsible for RopeFest Moscow, while RopeFest Petersburg 2019 billed Master Set as a Moscow participant whose bondage practice began with self-study in the mid-2000s before turning toward dynamic suspensions. RopeFest Moscow 2023 channel archives describe Set / Master Set as a bondage artist, sadist and kinkster, co-organizer and regular host of RopeDay events in Moscow, practicing rope bondage since 2007, presenting stage shows, public actions, workshops and private teaching. ### Miss Gen

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/miss-gen/

Transmission — Miss Gen is an Australian teacher in the Kazami-ryū.

Fact — Era: 2010s

Transmission — TEACHER · PERFORMER · ORGANIZER

Transmission — Australia-centered Kazami-Ryu teacher, performer, organizer and Japanese interpreter publicly appearing as Miss Gen / MissEvilGen, centered on Evil Girls HQ in South East Queensland. Evil Girls HQ reports that Miss Gen was appointed a qualified Kazami-Ryu instructor and deshi to Kazami Ranki in late 2015, and was announced in 2018 as Shihan / Head of School for the Oceania region; its 2025 cabaret page frames October 2015 as the point when she received deshi title and permission to teach Kazami's style in Australia, and says she built Kazami-Ryu into a large Australasia school with instructors in Brisbane, Perth, Auckland and Christchurch. Evil Girls HQ's 2020 intensive profile adds Australia / Japan performance context, Japanese-language interpreter work for Sydney Rope Festival 2013-2014, Kazami Ranki, Nawashi Kanna and Kasumi Hourai Australian tours, and Yoi Yoshida's O-Nawa 2014 in Japan. Studio Kink's 2019 Hourai Kasumi page uses the Miss Evil Gen spelling and characterizes her as an interpreter-grade Japanese translator and rope-aware educator. A 2025 podcast page gives current handles @MissEvilGen and @miss_gen_does_rope, and frames her as head of Evil Girls HQ with 25+ years rope experience.

Fact — School: Kazami-ryū

### Nawa Kenshin

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/nawa-kenshin/

Fact — Nawa Kenshin is a rigger.

Fact — Era: 2010s

Transmission — RIGGER · TEACHER · PHOTOGRAPHER

Transmission — Singapore-centered shibari artist, teacher, performer and photographer using the public identity Nawa Kenshin / Sean / @nawakenshin. His official about page says he began shibari in 2010 after SM and gravure photography / videography work, received guidance from Chris Thomson / Calendar King, renewed his practice in 2019, and later took lessons from Osada Steve, Akechi Kanna and Kasumi Hourai while drawing on Yukimura, Yagami and Osada-ryu style influences. The same page frames his current approach as Nawakokoro / intentional tying with heart. Current official services pages offer Singapore shibari experiences, lessons and treated jute rope services, while official blog posts document regular introductory shibari classes at 66 Chambers, Q1 2025 performances at Costica Singapore and Manila / ShibariPH teaching plus rope-photography lighting classes. Shibari.PH independently frames him as a Singapore shibari artist and photographer teaching and performing in Manila in April 2026.

text:
### Nicolas Yoroi

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/nicolas-yoroi/

Fact — Nicolas Yoroi is a Belgian nawashi in the Osada / Western.

Fact — Era: 2010s

Transmission — NAWASHI · TEACHER · PHOTOGRAPHER

Transmission — Belgian rope artist, educator, rigger and photographer with 20+ years in rope. Aikidoka since 1997 — martial-arts background became his main influence for life and kinbaku. Spent four years in Japan studying under Go Arisue, dedicating himself to Aikidō, Japanese culture and the art of rope. Has also studied with Tesshin Doyama, Hajime Kinoko, Ottonawa, Kazami Ranki, Felix Ruckert, Shadow, and Pedro Cordas. Began teaching rope bondage in 2011; upon returning to Europe developed his own tying style, 'Yoroi-ryu'. Opened a Brussels dojo in 2015; the current Nicolas's Dojo site records a Brussels / Anderlecht dojo address and frames the dojo around rope art, self-study, dance, massage, martial arts, performance, power exchange and safety. His work integrates Aikidō, Thai massage, contact-improvisation and the System A approach. Recurring EURIX teacher, Zurich Shibari Masterclasses guest and European workshop presenter; collaborator with Felix Ruckert.

Fact — School: Osada / Western

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/ota-kinbaku/

Fact — OtaKinbaku is documented as an American rigger.

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — RIGGER · PHOTOGRAPHER

Fact — Bay Area / Oakland-centered rope artist, nawashi, photographer, performer and educator working as OtaKinbaku / Ota. His Tumblr and Bluesky profiles frame him as a Bay Area / Oakland rope photographer, rigger and Oakland Rope Salon founder. VoxBody's Oakland Rope Salon listing places Ota as host and characterizes the salon as a small discussion-focused Japanese rope-bondage gathering, with Ota's 2015 Japan lessons and time around Naka Akira and other bakushi treated here as influence / study context rather than formal apprenticeship. Filthy Studios records Ota in its educator / production-support crew, and Twisted Windows records Ota among its San Francisco Bay Area rope troupe performers.

### Red Sabbath

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/red-sabbath/

Fact — Red Sabbath is an Italian model.

Fact — Era: 2010s

Transmission — MODEL · TEACHER

Transmission — Italian rope model, performer and teacher. Life and work partner of Riccardo Wildties and later the main public model / teaching partner in Kinbaku LuXuria, a Naka-ryu-descended semenawa practice adapted for Western bodies and classrooms. A 2012 LuXuria Rome page records both RedSabbath and Gorgone as models for early private sessions; later public LuXuria / Peony Studio traces place Red Sabbath with Wildties in European teaching, performance and certified-educator context. Active with Wildties since the early 2010s; performed with him at Naka Nawakai in Tokyo in August 2014 and has taught spanning Europe. Current Peony Studio / TicketTailor workshop context exposes Alexandra Rouge / @alexandrarouge93 as a RedSabbath booking and contact alias. Former dancer and dance teacher whose rope work emphasizes body movement, surrender, and the darker emotional register of floorwork and partial suspensions.

### Riccardo Wildties

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/riccardo-wildties/

Fact — Riccardo Wildties, born 1976, is an Italian nawashi in the Naka-ryū.

Chronology — Born: 1976

Fact — Era: 2010s

Transmission — NAWASHI · TEACHER

Fact — Italian nawashi, photographer and educator. Initially self-taught, then turned decisively toward Naka Akira's semenawa after encountering Naka's work in 2012 and meeting him in Moscow in 2013. Early public traces and current atlas context place the first Kinbaku LuXuria period in 2011-2012 as a project co-established / co-run with Gorgone, while later public LuXuria sources frame Wildties and Red Sabbath as the main teaching / performance pair. Officially became Naka Akira's deshi in 2015 and is commonly described as Naka's ichiban deshi. Kinbaku LuXuria is listed as a Naka-ryu-descended style that re-engineers aspects of the technique for Western students and models while preserving its emotional and aesthetic core.

Fact — School: Naka-ryū

text:
### Ryuan (流庵殿)

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/ryuan/

Fact — Ryuan (流庵殿) is a Spanish nawashi in the Yagami-ryū.

Fact — Era: 2000s

Transmission — NAWASHI · TEACHER

Fact — Spanish nawashi and instructor centered in Gijón, Asturias. Active in the Spanish shibari scene since approximately 2004–2005, making him one of the most senior Western practitioners. Leads Nawakai Gijón / Escuela de Shibari, a Yagami-ryu dojo for in-person teaching in Asturias, and Shibari Dojo as an online Spanish-language school. Developed the #MSAFE teaching methodology for accessible rope education. A 2017 interview characterizes Osada Steve's 2010 Barcelona workshop as a positive influence on his perspective and preserves his Gijón organizing context around a Hajime Kinoko tour stop. Ren Yagami's official profile says Ryuan began studying under Yagami Ren in 2016 and became a certified instructor in 2018; a current atlas context gives the Japanese certification wording as 流庵殿 認定教師 鵺神流初傳.

Fact — School: Yagami-ryū

### Sakurada Denjiro (桜田伝次郎)

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/sakurada-denjiro/

Transmission — Sakurada Denjiro (桜田伝次郎), born 1952, is documented as a Japanese nawashi in the Akechi lineage.

Chronology — Born: 1952

Fact — Era: 1980s

Fact — NAWASHI · PERFORMER

Fact — Japanese nawashi and underground-theatre figure. Led the underground troupe Sekai Gekijō (Theater of the World) and established GSG Planning to organise SM shows. In the 1970s formed relationships with Shigeru Kayama, Eikichi Osada, Denki Akechi and Keiyu Tamai. In 1978 met Denki Akechi (with brother Shin Sakurada) at GSG Planning. Opened the small hall Theatre Poo in Shinjuku in 1983. Retired from performing in 1985, after which Akechi Denki took over his shows. The 'Den' in 'Denki' was borrowed from his given name Denjirō.

Transmission — School: Akechi lineage

text:
### Tsukioka Yoshitoshi (月岡芳年)

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/tsukioka-yoshitoshi/

Fact — Tsukioka Yoshitoshi (月岡芳年), 1839–1892, is documented as a Japanese ukiyo-e artist in the Foundational.

Chronology — Born: 1839

Chronology — Died: 1892

Fact — Era: 1800s

Fact — UKIYO-E ARTIST

Fact — Last great master of the ukiyo-e genre of woodblock printing. Born Owariya Yonejirō on 30 April 1839 in the Shimbashi district of Edo. Apprenticed to Kuniyoshi in 1850, receiving the name 'Yoshitoshi' in the Utagawa school lineage. His 'bloody period' (late 1860s) produced images of graphic violence and brutality — a key source for kinbaku visual culture, such as the muzan-e (cruel-images) genre. Suffered a nervous breakdown in 1872 and stopped working; resumed in 1873 under the new name 'Taiso' ('Great Resurrection'). His most celebrated late series — One Hundred Aspects of the Moon (1885–92) and New Forms of Thirty-Six Ghosts (1889–92) — coincide with an 1885 Tokyo Hayari Hosomiki ranking that placed him as Japan's number-one ukiyo-e artist. Died of cerebral haemorrhage on 9 June 1892, aged 53.

Fact — School: Foundational

### Unnamedminor

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/unnamedminor/

Fact — Unnamedminor is a Thai nawashi.

Fact — Era: 2010s

Transmission — NAWASHI · TEACHER · PERFORMER

Transmission — Bangkok-centered Thai shibari artist, performer, rigger and teacher using the public identity Unnamedminor / Minor. Khaosod's 2018 Bangkok scene report names Phetcharada "Minor" Pacharee as one of the few Thai suspension-capable shibari practitioners and says she helped bring aerial suspension into public performance in Bangkok. Wonderfruit's 2020 interview names her as Phetcharada Pacharee / Unnamedminor, characterizes a live Wonderfruit 2019 appearance, and says she started studying and tying herself around 2016 before studying with foreign teachers in Asian and European styles. GroundControl, Time Out / Zipevent and Kinky Bangkok document later art, exhibition, teaching and workshop contexts, while public social surfaces frame the work around shibari art, photoshoots, private sessions and shibari + music performance in Bangkok. A 2026 public Instagram profile screenshot links the Mino Shibari / @mino.shibari profile back to the same @Unnamedminorxxx X route and records @mino_r.shibari as backup, so the Mino handles are listed as current Unnamedminor contact / alias context rather than a separate person.

### Xa Na

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/xa-na/

Fact — Xa Na is an Italian bodyworker.

Fact — Era: 2010s

Transmission — BODYWORKER · TEACHER

Transmission — Italian / Berlin-Rome bodyworker, BDSM practitioner, teacher and kinbaku performer. Melania Mieli's 2019 interview characterizes Xa Na as a Shiatsu expert since 2000, researching the fusion of bodywork and BDSM, and presenting workshops at Xplore Rome on Shiatsu, shibari, Hara/bodywork and creative action. The interview also notes a Moscow Knot bondage-festival performance. EURIX 2019 presenter and recurring 2020s beginner-course teacher.

### Zu

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/people/zu-pendragon/

Fact — Zu is a Dutch rope model.

Fact — Era: 2020s

Transmission — ROPE MODEL · CO-TEACHER

Transmission — Netherlands-centered rope model, rope bottom, self-tier and co-teacher using the public identity Zu / Pendragon in the Haag and Zu teaching context. The Bi+ organizer bio says Pendragon/Zu became fascinated by rope in 2019, practices regularly as a rope bottom and self-tier, is gradually also tying as a rigger, and focuses on rope-bottoming education, informed consent, neurodivergence and mental-health-aware teaching. Public event traces list Haag & Pendragon on the Prague Shibari Festival autumn 2024 programme and in the EURIX XXV / Autumn 2024 archive, Haag / Zu as Pinse Camp Denmark 2024 co-teachers, and Haag / Pendragon as 2025 Amsterdam Mini Queer Shibari Festival workshop facilitators.

### Shibari.Guru

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/platforms/shibari-guru/

Fact — Shibari.Guru, 2020–, is a Russian platform in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2020–

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — Online shibari education and performance-video archive by Boris Mosafir. The site programming includes paid video lessons covering basics, patterns, training and suspension, and its About page says the course content is based on the RopeSchool course and Boris Mosafir's additional author seminars. The same page frames video lessons as supplementary study content to live training and says another major part of the project is performance-video documentation from ArtCo-organized events and recordings sent by other shibari masters. Its Shibari show section preserves 120+ full performance videos from RopeFest, RopeDay, Bondage Cafe SPb, Moscow Knot, Rome Bondage Week and other event traces.

### Espacio Nos

Source URL: https://shibariatlas.org/lineage/studios/espacio-nos/

Fact — Espacio Nos, 2020–, is a Spanish studio in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2020–

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — Barcelona interdisciplinary shibari / movement / art / performance space created by Pauline Massimo and began operating in 2020. The official about page frames Espacio Nos as a full-time physical space focused on LGBTQI+ / femininities / marginalized communities and as a Spanish pioneer in educational and entertainment shibari events with explicit anti-abuse policies. It is structurally useful for the map because its own archive and current program connect Barcelona to international shibari teachers and performers already in the map, such as Kasumi Hourai, Akechi Kanna & Kagura, Barkas & Addie, Gandalf, Tamandua, Hua Hua, Gestalta and Ceci Ferox.

Fact — Wisimi Studio, 2020s–, is a Polish studio in the bakushi / kinbaku ecosystem.

Fact — Active: 2020s–

Fact — Era: 2020s

Fact — Warsaw shibari / rope studio and community space visible via 2025 public event listings. A Ryhor Shabeka workshop listing places his semi-suspension class at Wisimi Studio in Warsaw and notes weekly practical rope classes led by Arkadiusz Hezler plus Tuesday rope jams. Fact — Founding-decade slice of BoundCon Munich, the international bondage and fetish convention first held at Kleine Olympiahalle in 2004. Official archived pages document BoundCon I-V via 2008, such as BoundCon III — Revenge of the Ropes, BoundCon IV — Back to the Future at Tonhalle / Kultfabrik and BoundCon V — Destination Bizarre at Zenith. Early rope programmes connected German and international show, workshop and exhibitor circuits via figures and projects such as Matthias T. J. Grimme / Bondage Project, Lew Rubens / BoundnDetermined and RopeMarks / Bob Roos. The first documented public convention was held in 2004; later 2003 origin wording likely refers to the project's foundation or planning phase.

Fact — Tokyo-centered annual female bakushi festival / high-skilled amateur and professional kinbaku stage established and produced by Yoi Yoshida since 2009. Kinbaku Today's Yoi interview says the first June 2009 Onawa Asobi grew from the Good-Studio orbit and featured Hajime Kinoko, Kasumi Hourai, Shigonawa Bingo, Ero Ouji and others; later interview traces place Ranki Kazami at Onawa Asobi 2010, while Shibari Lounge sources document BeShibari and Shibarista Jess performing at Tokyo Onawa Asobi in 2019.
